Feed failures back into design, document the change, and re-verify.
The evidence that outputs meet inputs - part of the design history file.

subs.why: Validation asks 'did we build the right device?' - does it meet the user's needs in real use. It is the evidence that the device is fit for its intended purpose.
subs.what: Validate that the device meets user needs and intended use, on representative product and users.

how.d: Production-equivalent devices with actual intended users, not engineers.

how.t: Validate in the real use environment
how.d: Or a realistic simulation of where the device will be used.

how.t: Confirm intended use is met safely
how.d: The device does what it claims for the patient population, under foreseeable conditions.
Validation feeds, and is fed by, the clinical evidence where required.
For the technical file - your proof the right device was built.

activities.statement: Run the software lifecycle to IEC 62304 (parallel with design controls).
activities.why: For software - SaMD or embedded - IEC 62304 defines the lifecycle that makes software safe: requirements, architecture, detailed design, implementation, integration and system testing, plus maintenance, configuration management and problem resolution, all scaled to the software safety class. Skipping these steps leaves software that cannot be shown to be safe or maintainable, and the technical file cannot stand.
activities.how:
1. Write the SRS (SWD-TPL-03) and architecture (SWD-TPL-04); identify SOUP.
2. Verify (SWD-TPL-11/12) and validate (SWD-TPL-13/14).
activities.what: Run the software development lifecycle per IEC 62304 at the determined safety class: software requirements, architectural and detailed design, implementation with unit verification, integration and integration testing, system testing, and the supporting processes - configuration management, problem resolution and software risk management - with the records each produces.

steps.d: confirm the IEC 62304 software safety class set in P1.7 (A/B/C). It scales everything below - higher class, more rigour. Document the rationale.
derive software requirements from the system requirements and the risk controls, and check they're complete and testable (5.2).
define the software architecture and detailed design, and identify your SOUP (off-the-shelf / open-source components) with their versions and constraints (5.3-5.4).
keep a list of every third-party/open-source component, its version and its known issues - this is the seed of your SBOM and a frequent audit focus.
build the software and verify units against the detailed design with documented acceptance criteria (5.5).
integrate the parts, run integration and system testing against the requirements, and record results and every anomaly (5.6-5.7).
control versions of code and documents so you always know exactly what's in any release - version control with discipline, not just a repo.
log, evaluate and resolve software anomalies through a defined process, feeding safety-relevant ones back to risk.
maintain traceability from each requirement to its test, so you can show every requirement is verified.

steps.t: Software requirements trace to architecture and to tests.
The SOUP / third-party component list is maintained with versions.
Software is assigned a safety class with documentation scaled to it.

records: Software requirements, architecture and design documents
Unit, integration and system test records
tips: Your safety class (A/B/C) is the most consequential decision in software work - it sets how much rigour everything needs. Classify honestly; auditors challenge optimistic low classes.
SOUP/third-party components are where audits dig. Know every dependency, its version and its vulnerabilities - 'we use some open-source libraries' is not an acceptable answer.
IEC 62304 demands a controlled, documented process - it does NOT forbid agile. You can be agile AND compliant (see AAMI TIR45); you just have to evidence the activities.
Anomalies you found and fixed are evidence of a healthy process, not something to hide. An empty bug log looks more suspicious to an auditor than a managed one.

lean.start: A requirements->test trace, a one-page architecture note with your SOUP list, and a tracked issue log - scaled to your safety class.
lean.evolve: Add formal configuration management, automated CI/test pipelines and deeper documentation as the software grows.

subs.why: Software requirements translate the device's needs into what the software must do - including the risk controls implemented in software. They are the spec your tests will check against.
subs.what: Define and review the software requirements, including functional, performance and risk-control requirements, traced to system level.

how.d: Including the risk controls that the software must implement.
What it must do, how well, and its safety-related behaviour.
So it can be verified objectively later.
Link each software requirement to its system requirement and forward to its test.

subs.why: The architecture shows how the software is structured and where the third-party (SOUP) components sit - which carry their own risks and must be tracked and assessed.
subs.what: Define the software architecture and identify and manage SOUP (software of unknown provenance) with versions and risk.

how.d: Components, their responsibilities and interfaces, scaled to the safety class.

how.t: Segregate by risk where it helps
how.d: Isolate safety-critical components so a failure elsewhere cannot defeat them (class C).
Every third-party / off-the-shelf component, with version and supplier.
What you require each SOUP to do, and the safety impact of its known anomalies.
How you will track new vulnerabilities/anomalies over the product's life.

subs.why: Disciplined implementation with unit verification catches defects at the cheapest possible point and builds the evidence trail IEC 62304 expects.
subs.what: Implement the software to the design and verify units against acceptance criteria, scaled to the safety class.

how.d: Follow the design and your coding standards.
Test units to a depth that scales with the safety class.
Peer or static review for safety-critical units.
Version control with traceable commits tied to requirements/anomalies.

subs.why: Integration and system testing prove the pieces work together and that the software meets its requirements - the evidence that the software is safe to release.
subs.what: Integrate the software and run integration and system tests against the requirements, recording results.

how.d: Confirm the pieces work together as the architecture intends.
Verify the software meets every software requirement.
Specifically test the software risk-control measures.
Every result traced back to the requirement it proves.

subs.why: Configuration management and a controlled release mean you always know exactly what you shipped, can reproduce it, and can manage the known anomalies you release with.
subs.what: Establish software configuration management and a controlled release with its records and known-anomaly list.

how.d: Know precisely which source, SOUP and tools make up each version - reproducibly.
A repeatable build with release records and version history.
The residual anomalies you ship with, each assessed for safety impact.
The evidence that the software was developed and verified per the plan.

activities.why: Most device incidents are use errors, not component failures, so IEC 62366-1 requires a usability engineering process that designs the user interface around the users, use environment and tasks and proves, through formative and summative evaluation, that the device can be used safely. Use-related risk feeds the ISO 14971 risk file. Skipping usability engineering is a common Notified Body finding and a real patient-safety gap.
activities.how:
1. Write the use specification & identify use-related hazards (HUF-TPL-01).
2. Plan and run formative then summative evaluation (HUF-TPL-02/03/04).
activities.what: Run the usability engineering process: a use specification (users, use environment, tasks), a user-interface specification, identification of use-related hazards, formative evaluation through development, and a summative (validation) evaluation demonstrating safe use - documented in a usability engineering file linked to risk management.

steps.d: write down who really uses the device (clinicians? patients? in a panic? with gloves? poor eyesight?), where, and to do what. Real users, not idealised ones.
identify the tasks where a use error could cause harm (wrong dose, misread result). These are what you must prove can be done safely.
capture the users, use environments and tasks in a use specification - the foundation of the whole usability file.
define the UI (screens, alarms, labelling, physical controls) against those tasks and users.
run small, informal formative tests during development and fix the use problems you find - cheap now, expensive after launch.
every use-related hazard you discover goes into the ISO 14971 risk file with a control - usability and risk are joined at the hip.
with representative users, run the formal summative (validation) evaluation proving the safety-related tasks can be done safely. This is the evidence the Notified Body wants.
pull it together into the usability engineering file - the single place that proves your device is safe to use.

steps.t: A use specification identifies users, use environments and safety-related tasks.
A summative usability validation is planned or performed against the use spec.

tips: Most device harm comes from use error, not broken parts. Usability engineering is patient-safety work, not a UX nicety - and skipping it is a very common Notified Body finding.
Test with REAL representative users, not your own engineers. Your team knows the device too well to use it like a stressed first-timer would.
Formative testing during development is where you find the cheap fixes. Discovering a fatal use problem in the summative study (or in the field) is the expensive way to learn it.
Usability and risk share the same use-error hazards. If your usability file and risk file don't reference each other, an auditor will notice the disconnect.

lean.start: A short use specification, the safety-related tasks listed, and a few informal formative tests.
lean.evolve: Add a formal user-interface specification and a full summative validation study as the device nears market.

subs.why: Usability engineering exists because use error harms patients as surely as a component failure. The use specification - who uses it, for what, where - is the foundation everything else builds on.
subs.what: Define the use specification: intended users, uses, use environments and operating principle.

how.d: Professionals, patients, lay carers - their characteristics, training and limitations.
The tasks each user performs with the device.
Where it is used (clinic, home, ambulance) and the conditions there.
How the device is meant to be operated to achieve its purpose.

subs.why: Finding the tasks where a use error could cause harm focuses your design and testing on what actually matters for safety.
subs.what: Identify hazard-related use scenarios and the user tasks that are safety-critical.

how.d: Where a user could make a harmful mistake in each task.
The use sequences that could lead to harm.
The tasks that must not go wrong - your test priorities.
Feed these into risk management as hazardous situations.

subs.why: Formative testing during design finds and fixes usability problems early and cheaply, before they are baked into the product.
subs.what: Run formative usability evaluations during design and feed findings back into the design.

how.d: A few people matching each real user group - not colleagues.

how.t: Test realistic tasks on early prototypes
how.d: Click-throughs or early hardware; script the safety-related tasks.
Watch silently; note every hesitation, error and near-miss - the urge to help hides real problems.
For each problem, capture what happened and why (label, layout, workflow).
Formative testing is a loop - change the interface, then run it again.

subs.why: The summative study is the formal evidence that the finished design is safe to use - that representative users can perform the safety-critical tasks without harmful error.
subs.what: Run a summative usability validation on the final design with representative users, demonstrating safe use.

how.t: Use the final design & real users
how.d: Production-equivalent device, representative users, realistic environment.
Confirm users perform the critical tasks without harmful error.
Investigate root cause; confirm the residual use-risk is acceptable.
The formal evidence of safe use for the technical file.

activities.statement: Stand up the ISMS and product security (parallel).
activities.why: Connected and software devices face cybersecurity threats that are both a security and a safety problem. ISO 27001 provides the management system to govern information-security risk, and IEC 81001-5-1 (with MDCG 2019-16) requires security activities built into the product lifecycle - threat modelling, secure design, verification and vulnerability handling. Without these, devices ship with exploitable weaknesses and the organisation cannot protect its own and patients' data.
activities.how:
1. Define ISMS scope & SoA (ISM-TPL-01); assess infosec risk (ISM-TPL-02).
2. Threat-model the product; build the SBOM (SEC-LIS-01); plan security testing.
activities.what: Stand up the information security management system (ISMS) and the product-security lifecycle: an ISMS scope, policy, risk assessment and Statement of Applicability with controls, plus product-security activities - threat modelling, secure design and verification, SBOM and vulnerability management - integrated with design controls and risk management.

steps.d: decide what the information security management system covers (which systems, data, sites). A tight scope you actually run beats a broad one you don't.
a short, leadership-backed statement of how you manage information security - the anchor for everything below.
identify and assess information-security risks to your data and systems, and decide how you'll treat each (mitigate, accept, transfer).
document which ISO 27001 Annex A controls you apply and why (and why you exclude any). The SoA is the heart of an ISO 27001 audit.
for the device itself, model how an attacker could compromise it, and design security IN (IEC 81001-5-1) - not bolt it on later.
turn the threat model into concrete security requirements the software must meet, traceable to the threats.
keep a Software Bill of Materials - every component and version - so when a vulnerability hits a library you know in minutes whether you're affected.
establish how you monitor for, triage and patch vulnerabilities, and how someone can report one to you (coordinated disclosure).
build the security controls into the software and verify them in the software lifecycle, traceable to the threats they address.

steps.t: A security risk assessment covers the device and its data flows.
An SBOM (software bill of materials) is maintained.
Security controls are documented (Statement of Applicability or equivalent).

records: ISMS scope, policy and risk assessment
tips: For a connected device, security IS safety - a vulnerability can lead to patient harm. That's why MDR and the FDA now demand it, and why it lives in your QMS.
An SBOM is non-negotiable now. When the next Log4j-style vulnerability lands, companies that know their components patch in hours; the ones that don't scramble for weeks.
The Statement of Applicability is the document ISO 27001 auditors live in. Every Annex A control gets an 'applied because...' or 'excluded because...' - vague entries get picked apart.
Don't bolt security on at the end. Threat-model during design - retrofitting security into a finished product is far more expensive and far less effective.

lean.start: A short security policy, a basic risk assessment, and an SBOM - a lean ISMS scoped to what matters.
lean.evolve: Add the full Statement of Applicability, threat-modelling depth and certification (ISO 27001 / SOC 2) as you scale.

subs.t: Define the ISMS scope & security policy
subs.why: A connected medical device or a company holding health data needs deliberate information security. Scoping a lean ISMS and writing a short policy sets the boundary and the intent without boiling the ocean.
subs.what: Define the scope of your information security management system and a short security policy.

how.d: Which systems, data flows and products it covers - keep it lean and real.
A signed statement of security intent and commitment.
Who owns security and who acts on incidents.
Measurable security objectives to steer the effort.

subs.why: Security risk assessment finds where you are exposed - the threats to the device and its data - so controls go where the real risk is.
subs.what: Assess security risks to the device and its data flows and decide on controls.

how.d: What is valuable - the device, its data, the supporting systems.
How an attacker could compromise confidentiality, integrity or availability.
Including impact on patient safety, not just on data.
From Annex A / secure-by-design measures, matched to the risk.
Security risks with safety impact also belong in the ISO 14971 file.

subs.why: An SBOM tells you what is in your software so you can react when a component is found vulnerable. Secure-by-design controls build protection into the product rather than bolting it on.
subs.what: Produce a software bill of materials and implement secure-by-design controls in the device.

how.d: Every software component and version, including SOUP, in a machine-readable form.
Authentication, encryption, least privilege, secure defaults, integrity checks.
A safe, authenticated way to patch it in the field.
Protect how the software is built and delivered.
Security controls that are also risk controls trace into the risk file.

subs.why: Security is never 'done'. Monitoring for vulnerabilities and having a tested incident-response plan is what keeps a fielded device safe over its life.
subs.what: Set up vulnerability monitoring and a security incident-response plan, linked to your CAPA and reporting duties.

how.t: Monitor your SBOM for new CVEs
how.d: Watch components for newly disclosed vulnerabilities over the device's life.
Detect, contain, eradicate, recover, communicate - with named roles.
A tabletop exercise so it works when it counts.
A security incident may be reportable and usually triggers corrective action.

activities.why: Beyond the baseline (P1.9), GDPR imposes concrete operational duties when you process personal and health data: maintain records of processing (Art 30), run data protection impact assessments for high-risk processing such as health data (Art 35), put processor agreements in place (Art 28), and build data protection in by design and default (Art 25). Health data is a special category, so the bar is high; failing here risks fines and loss of trust.
activities.how:
1. Build the ROPA (DPR-LIS-01); run DPIAs for high-risk processing (DPR-TPL-02).
2. Put DPAs/BAAs in place with processors (DPR-TPL-03).
activities.what: Make data protection operational: maintain the records of processing activities, run DPIAs for high-risk processing, establish Art 28 processor/controller agreements, implement data-subject-rights handling, and embed privacy by design into the product and processes.

steps.d: turn the P1.9 data map into a proper GDPR record of processing (Art 30): purposes, data categories, recipients, transfers and retention, for each processing activity.
identify processing that's high-risk (health data, large-scale, profiling) - this is what triggers a DPIA.
for high-risk processing, run a data protection impact assessment (Art 35): describe it, assess the risk to people, define mitigations. Health-data processing almost always needs one.
every vendor that processes personal data for you (cloud, analytics, support) needs a signed Art 28 data-processing agreement. No DPA, no sharing.
be clear who is controller and who is processor for each data flow - it determines who is responsible for what.
build a process to handle access, rectification, erasure, portability and objection requests within the legal deadlines (usually one month).
make data protection by design and default a standard requirement in your product and process design (Art 25), not an afterthought.

steps.t: Records of processing activities are maintained.
Processor / controller agreements are in place for third parties.
A DPIA is performed where processing is high-risk.

tips: Health data is special-category data - the strictest tier under GDPR. If you process it, a DPIA is almost certainly required, and 'we didn't think we needed one' is not a defence.
Every processor needs a signed Art 28 agreement BEFORE data flows. Adopting a new analytics or support tool without one is a common, easily-avoided breach.
Data-subject requests have hard legal deadlines (usually a month). Build the process now - scrambling to fulfil an erasure request manually across systems is painful and risky.
Privacy by design is cheaper than privacy by retrofit. Collecting less data and minimising by default removes whole categories of risk for free.

lean.start: Records of processing and processor agreements for your actual data flows, plus a DPIA where it is clearly needed.
lean.evolve: Add automated data-subject-request handling, privacy-by-design checklists and a privacy management system as data use grows.

subs.why: GDPR requires you to document your processing. Keeping the records of processing current is the operational heart of data protection and the first thing a regulator asks for.
subs.what: Maintain records of processing activities for the personal and health data you handle.

how.d: Purpose, categories of data and people, recipients, retention and transfers (Art. 30).
For each activity - health (special-category) data needs an Art. 9 condition too.
Update whenever processing, systems or processors change.
A regulator can ask for it - keep it audit-ready.

subs.why: Anyone who processes personal data on your behalf must be bound by a contract. Missing processor agreements are a common, easily-found compliance gap.
subs.what: Put data-processing agreements in place with every processor that handles your data.

how.d: Cloud, analytics, support, sub-processors - anyone touching personal data.
GDPR-compliant data-processing terms with each.
Safeguards (SCCs/adequacy) for any data leaving the EEA.
Confirm each processor's security is adequate before you rely on them.

subs.why: High-risk processing - which health data often is - requires a data protection impact assessment. Doing it shows you have thought through and mitigated the privacy risk.
subs.what: Run data protection impact assessments where processing is high-risk.

how.d: Health data, profiling, large scale or new technology triggers a DPIA.
What, why, and whether it is proportionate.
Identify the risks to individuals and reduce them.
Your evidence; consult the regulator if high risk remains.

subs.why: People have rights over their data, and breaches must be handled on a clock. Having a working process for both keeps you compliant when it counts.
subs.what: Set up processes to handle data-subject requests and personal-data breaches within the required timeframes.

how.d: Where access, erasure, portability and objection requests arrive.
Handle subject requests within the statutory timeframe (usually one month).
How a personal-data breach is spotted, assessed and contained.
To the regulator, and to individuals if the risk is high.
Maintain the records that prove compliance.

activities.statement: Run the AI lifecycle (parallel, if AI).
activities.why: An AI/ML component adds obligations on top of the device rules: under the EU AI Act a medical-device AI is typically high-risk, requiring a risk-management system, data and data governance, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight and demonstrated accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity. ISO/IEC 42001 provides the AI management system to govern it. Treating AI like ordinary software misses these duties and the model-specific risks of drift, bias and opacity.
activities.how:
1. Govern training/validation/test data and examine bias (AIM-TPL-02).
2. Validate model performance and subgroups (AIM-TPL-03).
activities.what: Run the AI/ML lifecycle: data governance (relevant, representative, quality-checked data), model development and validation, performance and robustness evaluation, logging and record-keeping, transparency and human-oversight measures, and post-market model monitoring for drift - integrated with the risk file and technical documentation.

steps.d: a medical-device AI is almost always 'high-risk' under the EU AI Act - confirm it, because that triggers the full set of obligations below.
make sure training, validation and test data is relevant, representative of the intended population and quality-checked. Document where it came from and its limitations (Art 10).
actively check for bias across the populations the device will serve, and document what you found and did. Unexamined bias is an ethical AND regulatory failure.
develop the model and validate its performance against defined metrics, including how it holds up across sub-groups (robustness, accuracy), not just on average (Art 15).
design how a human stays meaningfully in control - can review, override or stop the AI (Art 14). 'The AI decides' is not acceptable for high-risk medical AI.
provide the information and instructions that let users understand what the AI does, its limits, and how to use it correctly (Art 13).
build automatic logging/record-keeping so the AI's operation is traceable (Art 12) - essential for investigation and post-market monitoring.
set up post-market model monitoring and a Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) for how you'll update the model in a controlled way.

steps.t: Fold AI risk into the main file
steps.d: add AI-specific risks (drift, bias, opacity, automation bias) into the integrated risk file (P2.1) and the technical documentation.

steps.t: Training and validation data governance is documented.
Model performance is reported across the relevant sub-groups.
Human oversight and an override mechanism are designed in.

records: Model validation and performance / robustness reports
tips: Treating medical AI as 'just software' misses a whole regulation. Under the EU AI Act it's high-risk, with duties on data, transparency, oversight and robustness ordinary software doesn't have.
Bias isn't optional to check. If your model performs worse for some patient groups and you didn't look, that's an ethical and regulatory failure - and increasingly, a headline.
Plan how you'll update the model BEFORE you launch. A Predetermined Change Control Plan lets you improve the model without a new submission each time - design it in.
'The AI recommended it' is not a defence. Build genuine human oversight - a person who can understand, question and override the AI - and prove it.

lean.start: Documented data governance, a validation report with sub-group performance, and a clear human-oversight design.
lean.evolve: Add an AI management system (ISO 42001), drift monitoring and a predetermined change-control plan as the model evolves.

subs.why: An AI medical device is only as trustworthy as the data behind it. Documented data governance - provenance, quality, representativeness, bias - is the foundation of a defensible model.
subs.what: Document the governance of your training, validation and test data: provenance, quality, representativeness and bias management.

how.d: Where each dataset came from, and your right to use it.
Completeness, accuracy and labelling quality of the training/validation/test sets.
Does the data cover your intended patient population and use conditions?
Find dataset bias (demographic, acquisition) and reduce it.
Keep the test set genuinely held out to avoid leakage.

subs.why: The model's performance must be proven - and proven across the sub-groups it will serve, not just on average. Hidden sub-group failure is the classic AI safety trap.
subs.what: Train the model and validate its performance, including across relevant sub-groups, against pre-set acceptance criteria.

how.d: Acceptance criteria before you measure - tied to the clinical need.

how.t: Validate on the held-out test set
how.d: Not on any data the model trained on.
Catch sub-group underperformance - the classic AI safety trap.
Performance, robustness and limits, tied to intended use.

subs.why: High-risk AI must keep a human meaningfully in control. Designing real oversight and a working override is both a legal duty and a core safety control.
subs.what: Design human oversight into the device, including a meaningful override and clear communication of the AI's limits.

how.t: Design how a human stays in control
how.d: How a clinician monitors and can intervene in the AI's output.
A real, usable way to overrule the AI's recommendation.
Make the AI's uncertainty and where it should not be trusted visible.
So oversight is real, not nominal.

subs.why: Models degrade as the world shifts. Monitoring for drift and controlling model changes - ideally with a predetermined change-control plan - keeps a learning system safe over time.
subs.what: Set up post-deployment performance/drift monitoring and a change-control approach for model updates.

how.t: Monitor performance & drift in the field
how.d: Watch for degradation as the population or input data shifts.
Assess each update for risk and performance before release.
A predetermined change-control plan for foreseen updates, so you can improve without a new submission.
Confirm performance and safety hold after each model update.

activities.why: Computerised systems that run the QMS, design or production must do what they are supposed to, reliably and with data integrity. ISO 13485 requires validation of QMS software (4.1.6) and production / monitoring software (7.5.6, 7.6), and where electronic records or signatures are used, 21 CFR Part 11 applies. A risk-based CSV approach (IEC/TR 80002-2, GAMP 5) focuses effort where system failure would most affect product quality or data.
activities.how:
1. Risk-assess each system (INF-TPL-01).
2. Plan, test and report validation (INF-TPL-02/03/05).
activities.what: Validate the computerised systems flagged in the inventory (P1.6) using a risk-based approach: validation planning, per-system requirements and risk assessment, installation / operational / performance qualification or equivalent testing, and ongoing control of changes - with data-integrity (ALCOA+) and Part 11 controls where applicable.

steps.d: take the systems flagged for validation in the infrastructure inventory (P1.6) - that's your CSV worklist.
rank systems by their impact on product quality and data integrity, and decide how deeply to validate each (risk-based, GAMP 5 / IEC-TR 80002-2). Not everything needs the same rigour.
for each system, document what it's used for and the requirements it must meet - you can't test against a spec you never wrote.
assess what could go wrong if the system failed or gave wrong data, and set acceptance criteria accordingly.
test that it's installed right (IQ), operates correctly across its range (OQ) and performs in real use (PQ) - or leverage the vendor's evidence where justified.
apply access control and audit trails so records can't be quietly altered, and meet 21 CFR Part 11 if you use electronic records/signatures (ALCOA+ principles).
when a validated system changes (an update, a new config), assess and revalidate as needed - validation isn't a one-time event.

steps.t: Systems affecting product quality or data are identified and risk-ranked.
Validation depth matches each system's risk; vendor evidence is leveraged.
Audit trails are enabled where systems hold regulated data.

records: Validation plan and per-system risk assessments
Validation summaries and change / revalidation records
tips: Risk-based is the key word. You don't validate a calculator and a production controller to the same depth - match the effort to the consequence of failure.
You can often leverage the supplier's testing for commercial software - but you must decide and DOCUMENT that you're relying on it, and validate YOUR specific use. Don't just assume.
Data integrity (ALCOA+: attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, accurate) is a hot audit topic. Audit trails that can be switched off, or shared logins, are classic findings.
Software updates can silently invalidate your validation. Have a change-control trigger so a vendor's auto-update doesn't quietly break a validated state.

lean.start: Validate only the systems that affect product quality or data, to a depth matched to their risk - leverage vendor evidence.
lean.evolve: Add periodic revalidation, audit trails and Part 11 controls as your computerised systems multiply.

subs.why: You do not validate every tool - only the computerised systems that affect product quality or data integrity, to a depth set by their risk. Ranking them first stops you wasting effort or missing a critical one.
subs.what: From the system inventory, identify which systems require validation and rank them by risk.

how.d: Only those affecting product quality or regulated data need validation.
Higher risk (release decisions, patient data) = deeper validation.
Light for low-risk tools; full IQ/OQ/PQ for high-risk ones.
Use supplier validation where you can justify trusting it.

subs.why: A short validation plan scaled to risk keeps the effort proportionate and gives you a clear pass/fail target - rather than testing everything or nothing.
subs.what: Write a validation plan per system: scope, approach, acceptance criteria, scaled to risk.

how.d: What you actually need the system to do correctly.
What is tested and how, scaled to risk.
What 'validated' means for this system - the pass/fail.
Use supplier qualification, then add your own use-case (PQ) testing.

subs.why: Running the planned tests against your real configuration and use cases produces the evidence that the system does what you rely on it to do.
subs.what: Execute the validation against the plan and record the results.

how.d: As you actually use it, with representative data.
Especially the functions that affect product or data.
Objective evidence per the plan.
Document, assess and resolve anything that fails.

subs.why: A system validated once drifts as it is updated. Defining revalidation triggers and audit trails keeps it trustworthy over its life.
subs.what: Define when revalidation is triggered and maintain audit trails for systems holding regulated data.

how.d: Updates, configuration changes, or a periodic interval.
Where the system holds regulated records (e.g. Part 11 data integrity).
Assess each change before applying it.
Re-test the affected functions and update the record.

activities.statement: Evaluate and control suppliers (incl. data processors).
activities.why: What you buy becomes part of your device, so ISO 13485 (7.4) and the FDA QMSR require suppliers to be evaluated and selected on their ability to meet requirements, purchasing information to define what is required, and incoming product to be verified. Poorly controlled suppliers are a leading cause of nonconformity and recalls; for critical suppliers, controls must be proportionate to the risk.
activities.how:
1. Evaluate and approve suppliers (PUR-FOR-01; PUR-LIS-01).
2. Conclude DPAs/QAAs; define incoming verification.
activities.what: Establish the purchasing and supplier-control process: criteria for evaluating and selecting suppliers, supplier approval and a controlled supplier list, quality / technical agreements for critical suppliers, defined purchasing information, verification of purchased product, and ongoing supplier monitoring and re-evaluation.

steps.d: list every supplier whose product or service can affect your device - components, sterilisation, contract manufacturing, test labs, key software. These are the ones you must control.
a critical-component maker matters far more than your stationery supplier. Rank by impact on the device so you spend control effort where it counts.
decide how you judge a supplier (certifications, samples, audit, track record) proportionate to their risk, and write it down.
assess suppliers against those criteria, approve the ones that pass, and keep an approved-supplier list. Only buy critical items from approved suppliers.

steps.t: Sign quality agreements for critical ones
steps.d: for critical suppliers, put a quality/technical agreement in place that obliges them to tell you BEFORE they change anything - silent supplier changes cause recalls.
make purchasing information unambiguous - exactly what you need, including quality requirements - so you get what the device actually requires.
check incoming product or services meet requirements by a method matched to risk (inspection, certificate of conformity, supplier audit).
track supplier performance, route supplier nonconformities through CAPA, and re-evaluate suppliers periodically on a risk basis (links to P3.6).

steps.t: An approved-supplier list holds evaluation evidence for critical suppliers.
Quality agreements are in place with critical suppliers.

records: Supplier evaluation / approval records and approved-supplier list
Quality / technical agreements for critical suppliers
tips: What you buy becomes part of your device - your supplier's nonconformity becomes yours, and so does the recall. Control critical suppliers like an extension of your own process.
The clause that saves you most pain is supplier change notification. A supplier 'improving' a material without telling you has triggered countless field actions - get it in the agreement.
Don't audit everyone equally. A risk-based approach (paper review for low-risk, on-site audit for critical) is both compliant and survivable for a small team.
A certificate on file is not verification of every shipment. Decide what incoming check each risk level needs, and actually do it.

lean.start: An approved-supplier list and a simple evaluation for your critical suppliers only.
lean.evolve: Add quality agreements, scorecards and on-site audits for critical suppliers as the supply chain grows.

subs.why: Not all suppliers carry the same risk. Categorising them and setting evaluation criteria focuses control on the suppliers that can actually hurt your product or data.
subs.what: Categorise suppliers by the risk they pose and define the evaluation and control criteria for each category.

how.d: Critical (affect product or data) vs non-critical.
What a supplier must meet to be used.

how.t: Scale the control to the risk
how.d: More oversight and evidence for critical suppliers.
So nothing critical sits with an unmanaged supplier.

subs.why: Approving suppliers on evidence - not habit - is how you keep poor inputs out of your product. The approved-supplier list is the record that proves it.
subs.what: Evaluate and approve your critical suppliers and maintain the approved-supplier list.

how.d: Assess capability, quality system and track record.
Record why each supplier is approved - not habit.
Keep the ASL current; buy critical items only from approved suppliers.
The basis and interval for staying on the list.

subs.t: Put quality & data agreements in place
subs.why: A quality agreement nails down who is responsible for what - change notification, records, audits. For data processors it is also a GDPR requirement.
subs.what: Put quality agreements (and data-processing agreements where relevant) in place with critical suppliers.

how.d: Who owns quality, records and audit rights.
They must tell you before they change a product or process you rely on.
GDPR Art. 28 terms where a supplier handles personal data.
How problems are raised and what records they keep for you.

subs.why: Approval is not forever. Monitoring incoming quality and re-evaluating suppliers catches a drifting supplier before it becomes your nonconformity.
subs.what: Monitor supplier performance and re-evaluate them on a schedule and on change.

how.d: Check what suppliers deliver against requirements.
Nonconformities, on-time delivery, responsiveness.
Periodically and whenever a supplier changes.
Escalate, re-qualify or remove from the ASL.

activities.statement: Make CAPA and incident response operational.
activities.why: CAPA is the engine of improvement and the place auditors look hardest: ISO 13485 (8.5) requires corrective action to eliminate the causes of nonconformities and preventive action to address potential ones, with verification of effectiveness. Connected products add security-incident management (ISO 27001 A.5.24+) and GDPR breach notification (Art 33/34) on tight timelines. Making CAPA and incident response operational ties complaints, nonconformities, audit findings, security incidents and breaches into one closed-loop system.
activities.how:
1. Operate CAPA (CAP-SOP-01) with root-cause and effectiveness checks.
2. Stand up incident & breach response (IRP-SOP-01) incl. the 72h breach path.
activities.what: Make CAPA and incident response operational: a CAPA process (intake, investigation, root-cause analysis, action, effectiveness check) fed by complaints, nonconformities, audits and trends; plus security-incident management and a GDPR breach-notification process with the 72-hour clock and decision criteria.

steps.d: decide which problems become a CAPA (recurring nonconformities, complaints, audit findings, adverse trends) vs a quick fix. Not everything is a CAPA - but the systemic stuff must be.
record what happened, where and how big - a vague problem statement leads to a useless investigation.
take immediate correction to stop the bleeding (quarantine product, pause a process) before you investigate the root cause.
dig past the symptom to the cause (5-whys, fishbone). 'Operator error' is almost never the root cause - ask why the process allowed it.
implement corrective action to remove the cause, and preventive action where the same cause could bite elsewhere.
check the action was effective before you close the CAPA - did the problem stop recurring? Closing without this is the #1 CAPA finding.
route complaints, nonconformities, audit findings and trends into one CAPA system, and analyse the data for patterns you'd miss case by case.
detect, classify, contain, eradicate and learn from information-security incidents (ISO 27001 A.5.24-A.5.28) - and feed the lessons into CAPA.

steps.t: Handle personal-data breaches on the clock
steps.d: assess any personal-data breach and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours, and data subjects where required (GDPR Art 33/34). Pre-decide who makes that call.

steps.t: The CAPA log records root-cause analysis and an effectiveness check.
CAPA is reserved for systemic issues and effectiveness is verified before closure.

records: CAPA records with root-cause, actions and effectiveness checks
tips: CAPA is where auditors spend the most time, because it shows whether your system actually learns. A pile of open, overdue CAPAs says more than any policy document.
Effectiveness check is the step everyone skips and every auditor checks. A CAPA closed without proving the fix worked is just paperwork - and a guaranteed finding.
'Operator error' or 'human error' is almost never the true root cause. Ask why the process let the error happen and reach the cause you can actually fix.
Don't open a CAPA for everything - you'll drown. Correct small things on the spot; reserve CAPA for systemic or recurring problems. A CAPA system you can't keep up with is worse than none.

lean.start: One CAPA log with root-cause and an effectiveness check. Reserve CAPA for systemic issues; fix small things on the spot.
lean.evolve: Add trend analysis, formal root-cause methods and integrated security-incident/breach workflows as volume grows.

subs.why: CAPA is how your QMS learns. A clear procedure and a single log keep it focused on systemic problems rather than becoming a bureaucratic dumping ground for every minor issue.
subs.what: Adapt the CAPA procedure and open a single CAPA log, reserving CAPA for systemic issues.

how.d: Systemic or high-risk issues - fix small one-offs on the spot, don't drown CAPA.
One place to track every CAPA to closure.
Identify, investigate root cause, act, verify effectiveness, close.

how.t: Set the sources that feed it
how.d: Complaints, audits, nonconformities, trends, incidents.

subs.why: Fixing symptoms guarantees recurrence. Real root-cause analysis is what makes a corrective action actually corrective.
subs.what: Investigate each CAPA to its root cause using a structured method.

how.d: Clearly state what is wrong, where and how often.
Immediate correction to stop ongoing harm while you investigate.
A structured method (5 Whys, fishbone) - not the first symptom.
Address the root cause so it cannot recur.

subs.why: An action that is not checked may not have worked. Effectiveness verification before closure is the step that separates a real CAPA system from a paper one.
subs.what: Implement the corrective action and verify it was effective before closing.

how.d: Make the change everywhere it applies.
Apply the fix to other products/processes with the same cause.
Confirm with evidence that the problem stopped recurring.
Do not close on 'action done' - close on 'action worked'.

subs.why: For connected devices, a security incident or data breach is a nonconformity with a regulatory clock. Wiring these into CAPA keeps response fast and compliant.
subs.what: Integrate security-incident and data-breach handling into the CAPA/incident workflow with their reporting clocks.

how.d: How security incidents and data breaches enter the system.
Breach notification (72h) and device vigilance timeframes.
The incident-response loop, recorded.
So the same incident cannot recur.

activities.why: Devices and their QMS change constantly, and uncontrolled change is how validated states are lost and unintended consequences slip in. ISO 13485 requires design and development changes to be identified, reviewed, verified / validated and approved before implementation (7.3.9), and document changes to be controlled (4.2.4). The regulations add the notion of significant changes that can affect certification (MDCG 2020-3). A single change-control process keeps design, document and process changes coordinated and evidenced.
activities.how:
1. Operate change control (CHG-SOP-01) with impact & significance assessment.
2. Link AI changes to the PCCP envelope.
activities.what: Establish change management: a process to request, assess (impact on safety, performance, regulatory status, validation), review, approve, implement and verify changes to design, documents, processes and software - including whether a change is significant for the regulatory route - with configuration management to keep versions coherent.

steps.t: Make changes go through one door
steps.d: set up a single change-request route so nothing significant changes informally. Uncontrolled change is how validated states quietly break.
record what's changing, why, and who asked - the start of the trail an auditor will follow.

steps.t: Assess the impact before any work
steps.d: before doing anything, assess the change's effect on safety, performance, risk, the validation status and the regulatory classification. Most damage comes from changes made without this step.

steps.t: Decide if it is a significant change
steps.d: determine whether it's a 'significant change' that affects your CE certificate (MDCG 2020-3) or needs a new FDA submission. Get this wrong and you can invalidate your market authorisation.
review the change with the right disciplines and approve it BEFORE implementation - not after it's already in production.
make the change, then re-do whatever verification/validation it affected. A change to a verified design un-verifies the parts it touches.
update all affected documents, records, risk file and the technical file, so nothing is left describing the old state.
maintain configuration/version control so the released state of design, documents and software is always known - you can always say exactly what's in the current device.

steps.t: Changes are logged with an impact assessment before implementation.
Significant changes are assessed for regulatory notification.

records: Change review / approval records and significant-change determinations
tips: The 'significant change' determination is where small companies get hurt. A change you thought was minor can require Notified Body approval or a new 510(k) - assess it before you ship, not after.
A change to a verified design un-verifies the bits it touches. Re-running the affected V&V is not optional - skipping it is how regressions reach patients.
Update the technical file and risk file with EVERY change. The classic finding is a device that's been improved three times while its documentation still describes version one.
Resist informal changes 'just this once'. The whole point of change control is that there is no back door - one uncontrolled change can unravel a validated state.

lean.start: A single change-request log with an impact check before each change.
lean.evolve: Add formal change boards, significant-change assessment workflows and configuration baselines as the device and team grow.

subs.why: Uncontrolled change is how validated systems and safe designs quietly break. A single change-request log makes every change visible and decided, not accidental.
subs.what: Establish a single change-request log capturing every proposed change.

how.d: One place for every proposed change - no side-channel changes.
What, why, who requested it, and what it affects.
Route low-impact vs significant changes appropriately.

how.t: Require a decision before any change
how.d: Nothing changes without documented approval.

subs.why: The point of change control is to think before you act: what does this change touch - risk, validation, regulatory status - so nothing is broken downstream.
subs.what: Assess each change's impact on risk, design, validation and documentation before implementing it.

how.d: Risk file, design, validation, labelling, documents and regulatory status.
What must be re-verified, re-validated or re-approved.
Sequence, the evidence needed, and a rollback if it fails.
Decide with the impact assessment in front of you.

subs.why: Some changes are 'significant' and trigger regulatory notification or a new conformity step. Missing that turns a routine update into a compliance breach.
subs.what: Assess whether a change is significant enough to require regulatory notification or re-assessment.

how.d: Against the MDR/IVDR (and FDA) significant-change criteria.
Notified Body or competent authority, before or as required.
Some changes need a new conformity step before release.
Record why a change was or was not notifiable - auditors check this.

subs.why: A controlled implementation - with the re-verification the impact assessment called for - is what keeps the change from introducing new problems.
subs.what: Implement the change under control, complete the required re-verification/validation, and update the documents.

how.d: Following the controlled change plan, not informally.
Re-verify / re-validate exactly what the impact assessment called for.
Specs, traceability, risk file, labelling and version history.
Verify the change achieved its purpose without new problems.

activities.why: Internal audits are how the organisation checks itself before a Notified Body or the FDA does - ISO 13485 (8.2.4) requires planned audits at intervals based on status and importance, conducted by objective auditors, with findings driving correction and CAPA. A working internal-audit programme is direct evidence the QMS is monitored and improved, and it surfaces problems while they are still cheap to fix.
activities.how:
1. Build a risk-based annual audit programme (AUD-TPL-01).
2. Conduct audits with independent auditors; raise CAPAs.
activities.what: Establish the internal-audit programme: a risk-based audit schedule covering all processes and applicable requirements, trained and independent auditors, an audit procedure (planning, conduct, reporting, follow-up), and the link from findings to correction and CAPA.

steps.d: plan to audit every process across the cycle (usually a year), auditing the riskier and busier processes more often. A bare calendar with no rationale is itself a finding.
give auditors basic audit-skills training - gathering objective evidence, open questions, classifying findings. A competent external auditor is also fine.
assign auditors so nobody audits their own work. In a tiny team, swap (software lead audits document control, QMR audits design) or bring in someone external.
before each audit, define its scope and criteria and tell the process owner. Surprise audits are for inspectors, not for your own improvement system.
audit each process against the standard and your own procedures, gathering objective evidence (records, observations) - not opinions.
record findings as nonconformities (major/minor) or observations, with the evidence, and report to the process owner and management.
route findings into correction and CAPA and verify they're actually closed and effective. An audit with un-closed findings is worse than no audit.

steps.t: An internal-audit programme covers every QMS process over a defined cycle.
Auditors are independent of the area they audit.
Audit criteria and a checklist are defined for each audit.
Findings are recorded, graded and assigned to owners.
Corrective actions from audits are tracked to closure.
Audit results are fed into management review.

tips: Internal audit is your dress rehearsal. Every nonconformity YOU find and fix is one the Notified Body won't - run audits to find problems, not to look clean.
Independence is non-negotiable: nobody audits their own work. For small teams, cross-audit each other or use an external auditor for the area you own.
An audit programme with findings that never close is a red flag. Auditors check that your audits actually drive change, not just generate reports.
Audit against objective evidence, not impressions. 'I think it's fine' is not an audit; 'I sampled five records and two lacked approval' is.

lean.start: A simple annual audit plan and a checklist-based audit of each process, by someone not auditing their own work.
lean.evolve: Add risk-based scheduling, trained lead auditors and deeper sampling as the QMS matures.

subs.why: Internal audit is how you check your own system before a regulator does. A programme that covers every process over a defined cycle is what makes it systematic rather than random.
subs.what: Build an audit programme covering all QMS processes over a defined cycle, risk-prioritised.

how.t: Cover every process over the cycle
how.d: Each QMS process audited at least once per cycle (e.g. annual).
Audit riskier and previously-weak areas more often.
Competent in auditing and in the area.
Dates per process, kept as the audit programme.

subs.why: Preparation - clear criteria, a checklist, an auditor independent of the area - is what makes an audit objective and useful.
subs.what: Define the criteria and checklist for each audit and assign an independent auditor.

how.d: What the audit covers and what it checks against (SOPs, clauses).
To structure evidence-gathering.
Auditors do not audit their own work.
Scope and schedule agreed in advance.

subs.why: The audit gathers objective evidence; recording and grading findings is what makes them actionable.
subs.what: Conduct the audit, gather objective evidence, and record and grade the findings.

how.d: By sampling records, observation and interview.
Conformity to the SOPs and the standard.
Nonconformity (major/minor) vs observation.
With the evidence behind each finding.

subs.why: An audit with open findings achieves nothing. Tracking corrections to closure turns findings into improvement.
subs.what: Drive corrective action on findings and verify closure.

how.t: Assign owner & due date per finding
how.d: Someone accountable to close each.
Not just a quick patch - link to CAPA for systemic ones.
Confirm the cause is actually addressed.

how.t: Feed results & trends to management review
how.d: Audit outcomes inform the review.

activities.statement: Run management review and data analysis (parallel).
activities.why: Management review is where Top Management judges whether the QMS is suitable, adequate and effective, using analysed data - audit results, complaints, CAPA, supplier and process performance, post-market and regulatory inputs - and decides on resources, changes and improvements (ISO 13485 5.6, 8.4). It is the formal closing of the loop and a mandatory record auditors always check.
activities.how:
1. Analyse quality/security/AI data and trends (MRV-FOR-01).
2. Hold the management review and decide actions (MRV-TPL-01).
activities.what: Run management review and the analysis of data that feeds it: define the inputs, collect and analyse data on QMS and product performance, hold the review at planned intervals, and record the outputs - decisions on suitability and effectiveness, improvements, resource and change actions.

steps.d: pull together the numbers: audit results, complaints, CAPA status, nonconformities, supplier and process performance, post-market data, regulatory changes (8.4).

steps.t: Look for trends, not just totals
steps.d: analyse the data for patterns - is something getting worse? A rising complaint rate matters far more than this month's count.
compile the inputs against a standard agenda, including progress on the quality objectives and follow-up from the last review.
sit down at planned intervals and genuinely assess: is the QMS suitable, adequate and effective? This is leadership's job, not something the QMR does alone (5.6).
decide on improvements, changes and the resources needed. A review with no decisions is just a status report.

steps.t: Record actions with owners and dates
steps.d: capture every decision and action with an owner and a due date, and track them to closure into the next review.

steps.t: Management reviews are held at planned intervals with minutes.
All required inputs (audits, complaints, CAPA, KPIs, feedback) are covered.
Outputs include decisions, actions and resource needs.
Actions from the previous review are followed up.
Quality objectives and KPIs are evaluated against target.

records: Management-review actions with owners and dates
tips: Management review is mandatory and auditors ALWAYS check it. A missing or token review signals leadership isn't really running the QMS - and that colours the whole audit.
It must be driven by analysed DATA, not vibes. 'Things seem fine' is not management review; trends, metrics and CAPA status are.
The output is decisions and actions, not minutes for their own sake. If nothing changed as a result, the review didn't do its job.
Don't let it become an annual panic. A standing agenda and data you already collect makes the review a 90-minute routine, not a week of scrambling.

lean.start: A short, data-driven review meeting with minutes and actions - quarterly is fine to start.
lean.evolve: Add richer dashboards, KPI trending and structured input packs as the system grows.

subs.why: Management review only works if it sees the whole picture. Gathering the required inputs turns it from a chat into a data-driven decision.
subs.what: Assemble the review inputs: audits, complaints, CAPA, KPIs, feedback, regulatory changes.

how.d: Audits, complaints, CAPA, KPIs, feedback, regulatory changes, supplier performance, prior actions.
Digestible data, not raw logs.
For follow-up at the review.
So nothing required is missed.

subs.why: Analysing trends - not just listing events - reveals whether the QMS is working and where to improve.
subs.what: Analyse quality data for trends in product, process and system performance.

how.d: Complaints, NCs, CAPA, KPIs - not just this period's count.
Are the quality objectives being met?
What is improving, what is worsening, what is new.
Turn data into 'so what'.

subs.why: The review is where top management judges QMS suitability and effectiveness and commits to improvements - a required, minuted event.
subs.what: Hold the management review, judge effectiveness, and decide actions and resources.

how.d: Their presence is required, not optional.
Is the QMS working and adequately resourced?
Improvements, changes to the QMS or product, resource needs.
The review record, with owners.

subs.why: Outputs nobody tracks are wishes. Following up the actions is what makes the review drive real change.
subs.what: Track the review's actions to completion and carry them into the next review.

how.d: For each review output.
Don't let outputs become wishes.
Confirm actions actually happened.
Report status at the next review.

activities.statement: Set up regulatory monitoring & external-document control (parallel).
activities.why: Regulations, standards and guidance change, and the device's own medical device file must stay current and complete - ISO 13485 (4.2.3) requires a medical device file per device type demonstrating conformity, and manufacturers must keep abreast of applicable requirements. Without active regulatory monitoring, the technical documentation silently goes out of date and conformity lapses.
activities.how:
1. Maintain the watch-list (REG-LIS-01) and the external-documents register (DOC-LIS-06).
2. Assess the impact of changes and feed change control.
activities.what: Set up regulatory monitoring and medical-device-file upkeep: a process to track changes in applicable regulations, standards and guidance, assess their impact, and keep the medical device file / technical documentation current, feeding changes into change management.

steps.d: list the regulations, standards and guidance that apply to your device and markets - this is your watch-list. You can't track what you haven't identified.

steps.t: Set up a way to hear about changes
steps.d: subscribe to the right sources (official journals, standards bodies, MDCG, competent authorities, notified-body bulletins) so changes don't blindside you.

steps.t: Review the landscape on a schedule
steps.d: check the watch-list periodically and log what changed. A simple monitoring log is enough - but it must be done regularly, not once.

steps.t: Assess the impact of each change
steps.d: when something changes, work out what it means for your device, its classification, your technical file and your QMS.
update the medical device file / technical documentation so it always demonstrates conformity to the CURRENT requirements, not last year's.
feed any required updates into change management (P2.11) so they're implemented in a controlled, evidenced way.

steps.t: A watch-list of applicable standards and regulations is maintained.
A periodic regulatory scan is performed and logged.
Regulatory changes are impact-assessed against the QMS and product.
The standard versions in use are current or their use is justified.

records: Updated medical device file / technical documentation
tips: Regulations don't stand still - MDR transition timelines, new MDCG guidance, updated standards. A device compliant two years ago may not be today if nobody's watching.
The medical device file is a living thing. The classic finding is a technical file frozen at launch while the device, the standards and the guidance all moved on.
You don't need to read everything - you need a watch-list and a habit. A quarterly scan of YOUR standards and authorities beats trying to boil the regulatory ocean.
Standards have transition periods. When a harmonised standard updates you usually have a window - track the deadline so you're not caught using a withdrawn version.

lean.start: A watch-list of your standards and regulations, scanned quarterly and logged in one place.
lean.evolve: Add formal impact assessments and horizon-scanning subscriptions as your markets expand.

subs.why: Standards and regulations change, and you are responsible for keeping up. A watch-list of what applies to you is the basis for staying current.
subs.what: Compile a watch-list of the standards, regulations and guidance that apply to your device and markets.

how.d: Standards, regulations and guidance for your device and target markets.
The specific editions you rely on.
Who watches each source.
Which documents/processes depend on it.

subs.why: A scheduled scan stops you discovering a regulatory change too late. Logging it proves you are monitoring.
subs.what: Scan for changes to your watch-list on a schedule and log the results.

how.d: Quarterly is a sensible start; watch official sources and NB bulletins.
Sources and date - proof you are monitoring.
New or updated standards, regs and guidance.
Hand each change off to be assessed.

subs.why: A change only matters once you work out what it means for you. Impact-assessing each change turns monitoring into action.
subs.what: Assess each relevant change for its impact on your QMS, product and documentation.

how.d: On the QMS, product, technical file and labelling.
Transition dates usually apply - plan to meet them.
Actions, owners and timelines.
Where product or the file is affected.

subs.why: Standards and regulations are controlled documents too - you must use the correct versions and withdraw superseded ones.
subs.what: Control external documents so only current versions are in use.

how.d: Standards, regs and guidance you use, as controlled documents.
Know which version is in force.
So people use the right one.
From active use.

activities.why: If an outage, cyber-incident or disaster takes out the systems or premises the QMS and production depend on, the organisation must be able to keep operating or recover quickly - ISO 27001 (A.5.29-30) requires information-security continuity and ICT readiness for business continuity. For connected and software devices, continuity is also a patient-safety and obligation-to-supply concern.
activities.how:
1. Do the business impact analysis (BCP-TPL-01): RTO/RPO.
2. Write and test the BC/DR plan (BCP-TPL-02; BCP-FOR-01).
activities.what: Establish business continuity and disaster recovery: a business-impact analysis of critical processes and systems, continuity and recovery plans with defined RTO/RPO, backup and redundancy measures, and testing of the plans.

steps.d: identify the processes and systems you can't operate without (production, document control, the eQMS, key data). These are what continuity protects.
for each critical thing, decide the most downtime you can tolerate (Recovery Time Objective) and the most data you can afford to lose (Recovery Point Objective).

steps.t: Plan how you keep going or recover
steps.d: write continuity and disaster-recovery procedures - what you do if a system, site or supplier goes down - proportionate to the impact.
put backups, redundancy and alternatives in place to meet your RTO/RPO. A single point of failure with no fallback is a continuity gap.
run an exercise - restore a backup, simulate an outage. An untested plan fails exactly when you need it. Feed the lessons into CAPA.
link continuity to the ISMS and incident management (P2.5, P2.10) so it's governed and audited like any other control.

steps.t: Critical systems and their recovery requirements are identified.
Backups are tested and a continuity plan is documented.
The continuity plan has been exercised at least once.

tips: An untested backup is a hope, not a control. The only way to know you can recover is to actually restore - test it before you need it for real.
Continuity is also an obligation to supply. If patients depend on your device, an outage that stops you supplying it is a safety and regulatory issue, not just an inconvenience.
RTO and RPO turn 'we should be resilient' into measurable targets you can design and test against. Without them, continuity plans drift into wishful thinking.
Don't gold-plate. A small SaaS-based company's continuity plan can be a page or two - the point is that you've thought it through and tested it, not that it's thick.

lean.start: Tested backups and a one-page 'what we do if X goes down' plan for your critical systems.
lean.evolve: Add a full business-impact analysis, RTO/RPO targets and regular continuity exercises as you scale.

subs.why: Continuity planning starts with knowing what you cannot afford to lose and how fast you need it back. Without that, a plan is guesswork.
subs.what: Identify the critical systems and data and their recovery requirements.

how.d: What the business and product depend on.
How fast you need recovery (RTO) and how much data loss is tolerable (RPO).
What could take them down (failure, fire, ransomware).
Effort follows the most critical, most exposed systems.

subs.t: Set up backups & a continuity plan
subs.why: Tested backups and a simple 'what we do if X goes down' plan are the core of resilience for a small organisation.
subs.what: Establish backups and a documented continuity/recovery plan for the critical systems.

how.d: On a schedule matched to your recovery-point target.
What we do if a given system goes down - simple and actionable.
Protected from the same failure.
How to actually restore, step by step.

subs.why: A plan with no owners stalls in a crisis. Assigning recovery responsibilities makes it actionable.
subs.what: Assign clear responsibilities for invoking and executing the recovery plan.

how.d: Who decides to invoke the plan.
Who restores what.
Staff, suppliers and providers needed in a crisis.
It is no use only on the system that is down.

subs.why: A plan you have never tested is a hope. Exercising it - even a tabletop - finds the gaps before a real incident does.
subs.what: Test the continuity/recovery plan at least once and fix what the test reveals.

how.d: A tabletop walk-through or a real restore test.
Gaps, missing steps, wrong contacts.
Update the plan and the backups.
Confidence decays - repeat it.

activities.why: Competence is not a one-off - ISO 13485 (6.2) requires the organisation to evaluate the effectiveness of training and the ongoing competence of staff, and to maintain awareness. Maturing competence means moving from 'training delivered' to 'competence demonstrated and effective', which is what keeps quality stable as people and processes evolve.
activities.how:
1. Evaluate effectiveness (HRT-FOR-02) and re-train where needed.
2. Maintain a current competence/training matrix (HRT-FOR-04).
activities.what: Mature the competence system: evaluate the effectiveness of training already delivered, reassess competence against current role requirements, address gaps, and refresh awareness - keeping the training records and competence matrix current.

steps.d: for training already delivered, evaluate whether it produced real competence (a supervised task, a short assessment) - not just that it happened.
as processes and the device evolve, re-assess each person's competence against their CURRENT role and spot new gaps.
deliver further training or mentoring for the gaps you find, and schedule it. A known gap with no plan is a finding.
refresh everyone's awareness of quality, safety and regulatory responsibilities - it fades, especially as new people join.

steps.t: Keep the records and matrix current
steps.d: update the competence matrix and training records so they reflect today's team and today's roles.

steps.t: Training effectiveness is evaluated, not just attendance.
Re-training and refresh needs are identified periodically.
Cover or succession for critical roles is considered.

records: Records of further training and awareness
tips: 'Training delivered' is not 'competence achieved'. This activity is about proving training WORKED - exactly what auditors check and most companies skip.
Competence drifts as the device and processes change. Someone competent for v1 may have gaps for v2 - re-assessment keeps the wheels on.
New starters are the most common competence finding. A robust onboarding-to-competence path (and its records) saves you here.
Watch single points of failure. If only one person can run a critical process, that's both a competence gap to close and a continuity risk (P3.4).

lean.start: A quick check that training worked and an up-to-date competence table.
lean.evolve: Add periodic re-assessment, development plans and succession cover as the team grows.

subs.why: Attendance is not competence. Evaluating whether training actually worked is what makes your competence claims credible.
subs.what: Evaluate whether training achieved the required competence, not just that it happened.

how.d: The competence the training was meant to deliver.
Can they now perform the task safely?
Assessment, observation, or work results.
Not just that training happened.

subs.why: The competence matrix is your live picture of who can do what. Keeping it current is how you spot and cover gaps.
subs.what: Keep the competence matrix current as people, roles and skills change.

how.d: Who is competent for what, live.
Quality-critical tasks without competent cover.
Update as the team and work change.
The matrix drives the training plan.

subs.why: Skills decay and requirements evolve. Periodically identifying re-training keeps competence from quietly lapsing.
subs.what: Identify re-training and development needs on a periodic basis.

how.d: Against current requirements and after changes.
For skills that have decayed or requirements that changed.
Quality, regulatory and security awareness on a cycle.
Growth for key staff.

subs.why: Single points of failure in people are a real risk. Planning cover for critical roles keeps the QMS running when someone leaves.
subs.what: Plan cover or succession for critical roles.

how.d: Where losing one person would hurt the QMS.
So critical tasks have cover.
For the QMR and other critical roles.
In both people and knowledge.

activities.why: Approving a supplier once is not enough - ISO 13485 (7.4.1) requires suppliers to be monitored and re-evaluated, with action when they fail to meet requirements. Ongoing supplier surveillance catches drift in quality, change in ownership or process, and emerging supply risk before it reaches the product.
activities.how:
1. Monitor supplier performance.
2. Re-evaluate by criticality and update the list (PUR-LIS-01).
activities.what: Run ongoing supplier surveillance and re-evaluation: monitor supplier performance against agreed metrics, conduct periodic re-evaluation or audits on a risk basis, manage supplier changes and nonconformities, and update supplier status.

steps.d: monitor each critical supplier's quality and delivery against the metrics you agreed - on-time, in-spec, responsiveness. A scorecard makes drift visible.
re-evaluate or re-audit critical suppliers periodically on a risk basis - approval isn't forever.
if a supplier changes process, site or ownership, reassess them. Those changes are exactly when quality slips.
raise supplier nonconformities, push corrective action, and adjust their approved status (probation, removal) if they don't improve.
keep the approved-supplier list up to date so buyers always know who's cleared to supply critical items.

steps.t: Critical-supplier performance is reviewed on a schedule.
Suppliers are re-approved when they materially change.
Supplier nonconformities are tracked to resolution.

tips: Suppliers drift. The one that was excellent at qualification can slide - performance monitoring catches it before a bad batch reaches your device.
A supplier changing ownership or moving a factory is a classic trigger for quality problems. Make it a mandatory re-assessment, not an afterthought.
Match surveillance to risk. Your critical steriliser gets close attention; a low-risk supplier gets a light touch. Auditing all suppliers equally exhausts a small team for no benefit.
If a supplier keeps missing requirements, escalate - probation or removal. An approved list that never changes despite poor performance isn't a control.

lean.start: A simple performance check on your critical suppliers and re-approval when they change.
lean.evolve: Add scorecards, scheduled re-audits and supplier-development programmes as the supply base grows.

subs.why: Approval is a snapshot; performance drifts. Monitoring critical suppliers catches a slipping supplier before it becomes your problem.
subs.what: Monitor the performance of critical suppliers against agreed measures.

how.d: Quality, on-time delivery, responsiveness.
A simple recurring rating.
What their deliveries actually show.
Before they become your nonconformity.

subs.why: Re-evaluating suppliers on a schedule and when they change keeps approval meaningful over time.
subs.what: Re-evaluate critical suppliers periodically and when they materially change.

how.d: Critical suppliers on a defined interval.
When the supplier changes product, process or ownership.
Evidence for keeping (or removing) them.
Reflect the outcome.

subs.why: Supplier nonconformities are early warnings. Tracking them and feeding CAPA keeps poor inputs from recurring.
subs.what: Track supplier nonconformities and drive corrective action.

how.d: From incoming checks and from use.
Hold them to a response.
Confirm it worked.
And reflect repeat offenders in re-evaluation.

activities.statement: Complete AI transparency, oversight, logging & PCCP.
activities.why: For an AI medical device, the EU AI Act adds obligations that culminate near market entry: record-keeping and logging (Art 12), transparency and instructions for use (Art 13), human-oversight design (Art 14), and registration plus the Annex IV technical documentation. Completing these makes the AI conformant and traceable, and is checked at conformity assessment.
activities.how:
1. Define transparency & human-oversight measures (AIM-TPL-05).
2. Design logging (AIM-TPL-07) and the PCCP (AIM-TPL-04).
activities.what: Complete the AI transparency, human-oversight, logging and registration obligations: finalise the transparency information and instructions, the human-oversight measures, the automatic logging / record-keeping, and the EU-database registration and Annex IV technical documentation for the AI system.

steps.d: complete the information and instructions that let deployers use the AI correctly and understand what it can and can't do, and its limitations (Art 13).
finalise and verify how a human stays in control of the AI - review, override, stop (Art 14) - and prove it actually works.

steps.t: Confirm logging is on and retained
steps.d: check the automatic logging/record-keeping (Art 12) is running and records are kept, feeding your post-market monitoring.
state the populations, conditions and inputs where the AI should and shouldn't be used - over-claiming is both a safety and a compliance risk.
complete the EU AI Act technical documentation (Annex IV) for the AI system, alongside the device technical file.
complete the required EU-database registration for the high-risk AI system (Art 49/71) before placing it on the market.

steps.t: The instructions for use state the AI's purpose, limits and intended users.
A working human-override is documented and tested.
Logging is enabled to support traceability.
Required transparency / registration information is prepared.

records: Transparency information and instructions for use
Annex IV technical documentation and registration records
