tips: These AI obligations are checked at conformity assessment - incomplete transparency, oversight or registration can block your market entry just as surely as a device gap.
Transparency means honest limitations, not marketing. Stating where the AI shouldn't be used protects patients and protects you - over-claiming is a fast route to harm and liability.
Human oversight must be MEANINGFUL - a person who can actually understand and override, not a rubber-stamp 'confirm' button. Auditors and the public both test this.
The AI Act registration and Annex IV documentation are IN ADDITION to your MDR/IVDR technical file, not instead of it. Plan for both.

lean.start: Clear instructions and limitations, a working human-override, and logging switched on.
lean.evolve: Add formal Annex IV documentation depth and registration workflows as the AI nears market.

subs.why: High-risk AI must tell users what it does, its limits and how to use it safely - a legal transparency duty and a core safety control.
subs.what: Complete the instructions for use and transparency information for the AI.

how.d: What the AI does and does not do.
Accuracy, confidence and known limitations.
How to use it safely and when to overrule it.
And the competence they need.

subs.why: Oversight and override must actually work, not just exist on paper. Confirming them keeps a human meaningfully in control.
subs.what: Confirm the human-oversight design and test that the override works.

how.d: A human can monitor and intervene meaningfully.
It actually overrules the AI in practice.
What happens when the AI is uncertain or wrong.
Oversight and override work as designed.

subs.why: Logging is what lets you investigate and prove behaviour after the fact - required for high-risk AI and invaluable in an incident.
subs.what: Enable logging that supports traceability of the AI's operation.

how.d: Inputs, outputs and key events, automatically.
Tamper-evident and kept long enough.
So you can reconstruct what happened in an incident.
Logging of health data still follows GDPR.

subs.why: Bringing the AI to market needs its documentation and, for foreseen changes, a predetermined change-control plan so updates don't each need a full re-assessment.
subs.what: Prepare the conformity documentation and a predetermined change-control plan for foreseen updates.

how.d: EU AI Act Annex IV documentation.
AI Act and MDR/IVDR evidence as one coherent file.
In the required databases/registries.
A predetermined change-control plan for foreseen model updates.

activities.why: A device may only be placed on the market if its clinical (MDR) or performance (IVDR) evidence shows it meets the safety and performance requirements for its intended purpose. MDR Art 61 and Annex XIV require a clinical evaluation; IVDR Annex XIII requires a performance evaluation (scientific validity, analytical and clinical performance). This evidence is the heart of the technical file and the most scrutinised part of conformity assessment.
activities.how:
1. Complete the CER (CLE) or PER (PEV) with conclusions traceable to data.
2. Produce the SSCP/SSP.
activities.what: Finalise the clinical evaluation (MDR) or performance evaluation (IVDR): the evaluation plan, the appraisal of clinical / scientific literature and any investigations or performance studies, the demonstration of safety and performance and an acceptable benefit-risk, and the evaluation report that feeds the technical file and PMS/PMCF.

steps.d: write the clinical (MDR) or performance (IVDR) evaluation plan: your intended purpose, the safety and performance claims you must back up, and HOW you'll get evidence.
decide whether you'll rely on published literature, equivalence to an existing device, your own clinical/performance data, or a mix - and be honest about what's actually available.
do a documented, reproducible literature search (a method like PICO) - not a quick Google. The Notified Body checks your search was thorough and unbiased.
critically appraise each source for relevance and quality against your claims. Cherry-picking favourable papers is exactly what reviewers look for.

steps.t: Run a study if there is a gap
steps.d: if literature and equivalence don't cover your claims, plan and run a clinical investigation (ISO 14155) or performance study to fill the gap.
pull the evidence together to show the device meets the safety and performance requirements and that its benefits outweigh its residual risks for the intended purpose.
compile the Clinical (or Performance) Evaluation Report - the centrepiece of your technical file and the most scrutinised document you'll write.
connect the evaluation to post-market surveillance and PMCF/PMPF so it's kept alive with real-world data, not frozen at submission.

steps.t: A clinical/performance evaluation plan defines the route and acceptance criteria.
A documented, reproducible literature search and appraisal exists.
Clinical evidence supports each claim and the intended purpose.

tips: The clinical/performance evaluation is the single most scrutinised part of MDR/IVDR conformity assessment. Thin or biased evidence here is the most common reason devices fail review.
Your literature search must be systematic and reproducible. A reviewer who can't follow how you searched - or spots you skipped unfavourable results - will reject the whole evaluation.
Equivalence is much harder under MDR than it used to be. Claiming equivalence to another company's device, without access to its technical data, rarely survives - plan for your own evidence.
The CER is never 'finished' - it's a living document fed by PMS and PMCF. Build the update loop now, because the Notified Body checks it's maintained.

lean.start: A clinical/performance evaluation plan and a documented literature search and appraisal against your claims.
lean.evolve: Add your own clinical/performance studies and continuous CER/PER updates from PMS as evidence accumulates.

subs.t: Write the clinical / performance evaluation plan
subs.why: The clinical evaluation is the evidence that the device is safe and performs - the backbone of your CE submission. A plan sets the route and the acceptance criteria before you gather evidence.
subs.what: Write the evaluation plan: the route, the claims to support and the acceptance criteria.

how.d: Literature, own clinical data, equivalence, or a combination.
Every clinical/performance claim needs evidence.
What 'sufficient evidence' means for each claim.

how.t: Scope the state of the art
how.d: The condition, alternatives and benchmark to compare against.

subs.why: A documented, reproducible literature search and appraisal is the minimum evidence base - and exactly what a reviewer scrutinises.
subs.what: Run a documented literature search and appraise the evidence against your device and claims.

how.d: Databases, terms and inclusion/exclusion criteria - reproducible.
So it can be repeated and updated later.
Quality, relevance and applicability to your device.
Judge what it shows, don't just collect it.

subs.why: Every claim you make must be backed by evidence - over-claiming beyond the evidence is the classic finding that sinks a submission.
subs.what: Map each claim to its supporting evidence and write the evaluation report.

how.t: Map each claim to its evidence
how.d: No claim without support - over-claiming sinks submissions.
Consistent with the risk file.
Plan to close them or qualify the claim (feeds PMCF).
The CER / performance evaluation report.

subs.t: Plan PMCF to keep it live
subs.why: The evaluation is a living document. Planning post-market clinical follow-up is what keeps the evidence current after launch.
subs.what: Define the post-market clinical follow-up that keeps the evaluation updated.

how.d: How you gather real-world evidence after launch.
PMCF closes the residual gaps the CER identified.
Field findings feed back into the CER.
Keep the evaluation a living document.

activities.why: Certification is not the end - manufacturers must proactively collect and act on real-world data throughout the device's life. MDR Art 83-92 (IVDR Art 78-81) require a PMS system, a PMS plan, periodic safety / PMS reports (PSUR / PMS report), and post-market clinical / performance follow-up (PMCF / PMPF), feeding vigilance, risk and the clinical evaluation. A live PMS system is mandatory and a key audit focus.
activities.how:
1. Run PMS and produce PMS report/PSUR (PMS-SOP-01).
2. Operate vigilance reporting (VIG-SOP-02) and PMCF/PMPF (PCF/PEV).
activities.what: Operate the post-market surveillance system: a PMS plan, proactive and reactive data collection, trend analysis, the PSUR / PMS report, and PMCF / PMPF studies - with outputs flowing into vigilance, the risk file, the clinical / performance evaluation and CAPA.

steps.d: write the PMS plan: what data you'll collect, from where, how often, and what triggers action. PMS is proactive - you go looking for signals, not just wait for complaints.
gather post-market data reactively (complaints, returns, incidents) AND proactively (literature, registries, user surveys, similar-device data).
analyse the data for trends and signals - a slow rise in a particular issue is exactly what PMS exists to catch early.
write the periodic safety/PMS report at the frequency your device class requires, summarising findings and actions.
plan and run post-market clinical (or performance) follow-up to keep your evidence current after launch - regulators expect ongoing evidence, not a one-off.
feed PMS findings back into vigilance (reportable events), the risk file, the clinical evaluation and CAPA. PMS that changes nothing is just data collection.

steps.t: A PMS plan defines data sources, indicators and review frequency.
Field data, complaints and literature are collected and analysed on schedule.
PMS findings feed risk, clinical evaluation and labelling.
Periodic safety / PMS reports are produced as required.

records: PMCF / PMPF plan and evaluation report
tips: PMS is proactive, not 'wait for complaints'. Under MDR/IVDR you must actively go looking for safety signals - a reactive-only system is a guaranteed finding.
The PSUR is on a clock tied to your device class. Missing the cadence is a straightforward, avoidable nonconformity - put it in the calendar.
PMS exists to CHANGE things. If your PMS reports never trigger a risk update, a CER update or a CAPA, an auditor will ask what the point of them is.
PMCF/PMPF is where post-market reality meets your pre-market claims. Plan it before launch - retrofitting follow-up evidence under a deadline is brutal.

lean.start: A PMS plan and a simple log of complaints, field data and literature, reviewed on a schedule.
lean.evolve: Add proactive surveys, registries, structured PSURs and PMCF/PMPF studies as the device spends longer in the field.

subs.t: Set up the PMS plan & data sources
subs.why: Post-market surveillance is a legal duty and your early-warning system. A PMS plan defines what data you watch and how often.
subs.what: Write the PMS plan: data sources, indicators and review frequency.

how.d: Complaints, field data, literature, registries, similar-device data.
What signals a problem and when to act.
Frequency proportionate to risk class.
PMS report / PSUR and when each is due.

subs.why: Collecting and actually analysing field data - not just filing it - is what turns surveillance into safety.
subs.what: Collect and analyse field data on the planned schedule and act on the signals.

how.d: From all the defined sources.
Not just count events - watch the trajectory.
Has a signal crossed the line?
Update them with what the field shows.

subs.why: Serious incidents and field actions must be reported to authorities on a clock. Missing the timeframe is a violation regardless of the event.
subs.what: Assess events for reportability and report serious incidents and field safety actions on time.

how.d: Against serious-incident / FSCA criteria.
Within the MDR timeframes - missing them is a violation regardless of the event.
FSCA and field safety notices where needed.
Close the systemic cause.

subs.why: Periodic safety / PMS reports (PSUR) are the formal summary of what your surveillance found - required and reviewed.
subs.what: Produce the periodic safety update / PMS report as required for your class.

how.d: Findings, trends and actions taken (PSUR / PMS report).
Is it still favourable?
Where the data demands it.

how.t: Submit/retain as required for your class
how.d: PSUR cadence depends on device class.

activities.statement: Compile the technical file and declare conformity.
activities.why: The technical documentation is the dossier that demonstrates conformity, and the Declaration of Conformity is the manufacturer's legal statement that the device meets the regulation - MDR/IVDR Annex II/III define the technical-file content, and MDR Art 19 / IVDR Art 17 require the DoC. Assembling a complete, well-structured technical file is what the Notified Body reviews; gaps here stop certification.
activities.how:
1. Compile the technical file (Annex II/III); confirm classification & GSPR.
2. Issue the DoC; assign UDI & register (LBL); release.
activities.what: Compile the technical documentation per Annex II/III - device description, GSPR checklist, design / manufacturing information, benefit-risk and risk management, verification and validation, clinical / performance evaluation, labelling and PMS - and draw up the EU Declaration of Conformity once conformity is demonstrated.

steps.t: Build to the Annex II/III structure
steps.d: assemble the technical documentation in the MDR/IVDR Annex II/III order: device description, GSPR, design & manufacturing info, benefit-risk, V&V, clinical/performance, labelling, PMS. A well-organised file is half the battle.
work through the General Safety and Performance Requirements checklist and, for each, point to the specific evidence and the standard used. This is the spine of the file.
make sure every GSPR has traceable evidence behind it - 'we comply' with nothing to show is the fastest way to a major nonconformity.
go through the file looking for missing or weak evidence and fix it BEFORE the Notified Body finds it. Your own gap-check is far cheaper than theirs.
confirm labelling and instructions for use meet the requirements (symbols, UDI carrier, warnings). Labelling errors are a very common, very avoidable finding.
assign the Basic UDI-DI and UDI and prepare the device for registration (EUDAMED).

steps.t: Draw up the Declaration of Conformity
steps.d: once conformity is genuinely demonstrated, prepare the EU Declaration of Conformity (MDR Art 19 / IVDR Art 17) - your legal statement that the device complies.

steps.t: The technical documentation follows the Annex II/III structure.
A GSPR / essential-requirements checklist links each requirement to evidence.
The Declaration of Conformity is drafted and consistent with the evidence.
The file is kept current under change control.

records: Completed GSPR checklist with evidence links
tips: The GSPR checklist is the document a reviewer lives in. Every requirement gets specific, traceable evidence - 'complies' with nothing behind it is the fastest route to a major finding.
Organise the file to the Annex II/III structure. Reviewers know exactly where things should be; a disorganised file makes them assume the content is disorganised too.
Do your OWN gap analysis first. Every gap you find and fix is one the Notified Body won't raise - and finding it yourself is far cheaper than a deficiency letter.
Don't sign the Declaration of Conformity until the evidence genuinely supports it. It's a legal statement with real liability, not a formality to rush at the end.

lean.start: The Annex II/III structure populated, with your GSPR checklist linked to evidence. Organised beats exhaustive.
lean.evolve: Deepen the evidence, add manufacturing detail, and keep the file live through change control after launch.

subs.why: The technical file is the dossier that proves conformity. Structuring it to Annex II/III makes it reviewable - organised beats exhaustive.
subs.what: Set up the technical documentation to the Annex II/III structure.

how.t: Build to the Annex II/III structure
how.d: The required headings - organised beats exhaustive.
Design, risk, clinical and manufacturing evidence.
Point to the source records.
A reviewer can find anything fast.

subs.why: The GSPR (essential requirements) checklist links every applicable requirement to your evidence - the spine of the technical file.
subs.what: Complete the GSPR checklist, linking each requirement to the standard and evidence that satisfies it.

how.d: Applicable, or a justified not-applicable.
The harmonised/consensus standard you applied.
The document that proves each requirement is met.
Any unmet requirement is a submission blocker.

subs.why: The DoC is your legal statement that the device conforms. It must be consistent with the evidence and signed by the manufacturer.
subs.what: Draft the Declaration of Conformity, consistent with the technical file.

how.d: With all the Annex IV required content.
Against the classification, evidence and file.
Legal manufacturer, and SRN/Basic UDI-DI where required.
By the manufacturer's authorised signatory.

subs.t: Keep the file live under change control
subs.why: The technical file is not frozen at launch - it lives through change control and PMS for the device's life.
subs.what: Establish how the technical file is maintained current under change control after launch.

how.d: Every change updates the file.
Field data flows into the file.
Keep it current, not frozen at launch.
Always know the current state.

activities.why: Customer-related processes must be controlled too - ISO 13485 (7.2) requires the organisation to determine and review the requirements related to the product (including regulatory and customer requirements and the ability to meet them) before commitment, and to manage customer communication. Maturing sales, tendering and support ensures the organisation only commits to what it can lawfully and capably deliver, and that field feedback flows back.
activities.how:
1. Operate contract/feasibility review (SAL-SOP-01).
2. Route support feedback to complaints/PMS (SUP-SOP-01).
activities.what: Mature the customer-related processes: determination and review of product / regulatory / customer requirements before order acceptance or tender, controlled customer communication (including advisory notices and feedback), and aftermarket support - integrated with complaints, PMS and CAPA.

steps.t: Check you can deliver before you commit
steps.d: before accepting an order or bidding a tender, review the product, regulatory, customer and delivery requirements and confirm you can actually meet them (7.2.1/7.2.2). Don't promise what the QMS can't back.

steps.t: Only sell what is authorised, where it is authorised
steps.d: market the device only in markets where it's cleared/certified, and only for its intended purpose. Off-label promotion and pre-clearance selling are serious regulatory breaches.
manage enquiries, contracts, complaints and field communications through defined channels, so nothing important falls through the cracks (7.2.3).

steps.t: Be ready to issue advisory notices
steps.d: have a route to push a Field Safety Notice / advisory notice to customers FAST if a safety issue arises - speed matters when patients are at risk.
route customer feedback and field issues into complaints, PMS and CAPA, so what sales and support hear actually improves the device.

steps.t: Order / contract review confirms requirements can be met before acceptance.
A customer-feedback channel is defined and monitored.
An advisory-notice / field-action capability is defined.

records: Feedback routed to complaints / PMS / CAPA
tips: Selling before you're cleared, or promoting an off-label use, is one of the fastest routes to serious regulatory trouble. Sales enthusiasm must stay inside the authorised intended purpose.
Contract/requirement review before commitment isn't bureaucracy - it stops you promising a delivery, spec or market you can't lawfully or capably meet.
Your sales and support people hear the earliest signals from the field. If that feedback doesn't reach complaints/PMS, you're throwing away your best early-warning system.
Have the Field Safety Notice mechanism ready BEFORE you need it. When a safety issue hits, the speed of your advisory notice can be the difference that matters.

lean.start: A simple order/contract review to confirm you can deliver, plus one channel for customer feedback.
lean.evolve: Add tender processes, advisory-notice workflows and structured aftermarket support as sales grow.

subs.why: Before you accept an order you must be sure you can actually meet its requirements - contract review is that check.
subs.what: Establish a simple order/contract review to confirm requirements can be met before acceptance.

how.d: Can we actually deliver what is asked, including regulatory ones?
Before accepting the order.
Agree changes before acceptance.
Evidence the check happened.

subs.t: Set up the customer feedback channel
subs.why: Feedback is a required PMS input and a source of improvement. One clear channel is enough to start.
subs.what: Set up a channel to capture customer feedback and route it into PMS.

how.d: A known route for customer feedback.
All feedback is a PMS input.
Feedback feeds surveillance and improvement.
Act on it and respond to the customer.

subs.t: Set up advisory notices & field actions
subs.why: When a fielded device needs a correction, you must be able to reach users fast. Defining the capability before you need it is essential.
subs.what: Define how you issue advisory notices and execute field actions.

how.d: How to issue an advisory notice / field safety notice.
Know who has which devices/lots, so you can reach them.
Field actions are often reportable.
Rehearse so you can reach users quickly when it counts.

activities.statement: Run a full internal audit + management review cycle; close findings.
activities.why: Before inviting a Notified Body, the organisation runs a full internal audit of the complete QMS and a management review to confirm the system is implemented, effective and ready - ISO 13485 8.2.4 and 5.6. This dry run finds and fixes nonconformities on your terms rather than the auditor's, and produces the evidence of an operating, reviewed QMS that Stage 1 expects.
activities.how:
1. Audit the whole IMS (AUD); hold management review (MRV).
2. Close all CAPAs from findings.
activities.what: Run a complete internal audit covering every process and applicable requirement, and a management review of the whole QMS: close out findings, confirm objectives and effectiveness, and formally judge readiness for conformity assessment.

steps.d: run a complete internal audit across every process and all applicable ISO 13485 and MDR/IVDR requirements - this is your full dress rehearsal for the Notified Body.
audit as if you were the Notified Body. Findings you raise now cost you a fix; findings they raise can cost you the timeline.
drive corrections and CAPA for every finding and verify effectiveness BEFORE the audit. Walking into Stage 2 with open findings is a needless risk.
run a management review covering the audit results, PMS, risk, CAPA and objectives, and formally judge whether the QMS is genuinely ready for conformity assessment.
decide, on evidence, whether to invite the Notified Body now or fix more first. Inviting them before you're ready burns money and credibility.

steps.t: A complete internal-audit cycle is finished before the certification audit.
Open findings are closed or risk-accepted with justification.

tips: This is your dress rehearsal - treat it like the real thing. The harder you audit yourself now, the fewer surprises the Notified Body finds later.
Don't invite the Notified Body to force yourself to finish. Going in with open findings or a half-baked system turns an expensive audit into an expensive failed audit.
The readiness decision is a real management-review output, on evidence - not a hopeful 'let's just go for it'. Document the basis for the go/no-go.
A clean full internal audit plus a documented readiness review is exactly the evidence Stage 1 wants to see that your QMS actually operates.

lean.start: One complete internal audit and a documented readiness decision before inviting the Notified Body.
lean.evolve: Repeat it as a mature annual cycle feeding recertification and continual improvement.

subs.why: Before inviting a Notified Body, you audit the whole system yourself - a full internal audit is your dress rehearsal.
subs.what: Run a complete internal audit cycle covering the whole QMS.

how.d: A complete cycle covering every process - your dress rehearsal.
To find what the Notified Body would.
Better you find them than the NB.
Before the external audit.

subs.why: A management review just before certification confirms leadership has looked at the whole system and judged it ready.
subs.what: Hold a management review covering the full QMS and certification readiness.

how.d: All inputs, including the complete audit results.
Is the QMS effective and the evidence complete?
And the resources to do them.
A minuted readiness judgement.

subs.why: A documented readiness decision - all findings closed or risk-accepted - is what tells you it's time to invite the Notified Body.
subs.what: Close audit and review findings and make a documented certification-readiness decision.

how.d: Or formally risk-accept with justification.
The technical file and QMS are consistent.
Nothing missing before inviting the NB.
The certification-readiness sign-off.

activities.why: Conformity assessment under MDR/IVDR Annex IX is a two-stage Notified Body audit. Stage 1 reviews the QMS documentation and the technical documentation to confirm the system is in place and the device is ready for the full assessment. Passing Stage 1 - or closing its findings - is the gate to the on-site Stage 2.
activities.how:
1. Submit the IMS and technical file for the Stage 1 review.
2. Address Stage 1 findings.
activities.what: Undergo the Notified Body Stage 1 audit: submit the QMS and technical documentation, host the documentation review, and respond to and close any nonconformities or observations raised.

steps.t: Pick the right Notified Body early
steps.d: choose and apply to a Notified Body designated for YOUR device type - and do it early, because capacity is scarce and the wait can be many months.
compile the QMS documentation and the technical file(s) into the package the Notified Body asks for, in their expected structure.
submit, and agree the audit scope, timing and which device(s)/processes are in scope.
support the Stage 1 review: answer questions, provide evidence and clarifications quickly. Responsiveness here sets the tone for the whole assessment.
record every nonconformity and observation they raise, with exactly what they want to see.
address Stage 1 nonconformities with real corrections and CAPA, and submit clear evidence of closure - this is the gate to the on-site Stage 2.

steps.t: The submission documentation is complete and organised for review.
Reviewer questions are tracked and answered promptly.

refs: Annex IX Conformity assessment (QMS + technical documentation)

records: Responses and closure evidence for findings
tips: Notified Body capacity is a real bottleneck under MDR/IVDR. Apply early - waiting until the device is 'ready' to even start looking for a Notified Body can add many months.
Make sure your Notified Body is designated for your specific device type and codes. Applying to the wrong one wastes precious time you can't get back.
Stage 1 is about whether you're READY for Stage 2. Treat its findings as a gift - they tell you exactly what to fix before the expensive on-site audit.
Fast, organised responses to the reviewer build credibility. Slow, defensive ones invite deeper scrutiny - the relationship with your Notified Body matters.

lean.start: A well-organised submission and fast, honest responses to the reviewer.
lean.evolve: Build an ongoing relationship with your Notified Body and a smooth surveillance-audit rhythm.

subs.why: Stage 1 is a documentation review. A complete, well-organised set is what gets you through it without delay.
subs.what: Submit the technical documentation and QMS documents, complete and well-organised.

how.d: Technical file plus the QMS documents.
Nothing missing or out of date.
Clear structure and navigation.
Follow their submission requirements.

subs.why: How fast and clearly you answer the reviewer's questions sets the tone and the timeline of the whole assessment.
subs.what: Track and answer the reviewer's questions promptly and completely.

how.d: One owned list.
Point to the document that resolves each.
Delays cost weeks on the assessment.
With rationale, where you differ.

subs.why: Closing Stage 1 findings before Stage 2 is what stops documentation gaps becoming on-site nonconformities.
subs.what: Resolve the Stage 1 findings before the on-site audit.

how.d: From the documentation review.
And any system gaps they reveal.
Show each finding is resolved.
So gaps don't become on-site nonconformities.

activities.statement: Notified Body Stage 2 - on-site audit; certify.
activities.why: Stage 2 is the on-site Notified Body audit that confirms the QMS is implemented and effective in practice and that the device conforms - the final gate to the certificate and CE marking (MDR/IVDR Annex IX). Closing any Stage 2 nonconformities leads to issue of the certificate, after which the manufacturer may affix the CE mark and place the device on the market.
activities.how:
1. Host the on-site audit; provide objective evidence of effective operation.
2. Close any CAPAs and receive the certificate.
activities.what: Undergo the Notified Body Stage 2 on-site audit: demonstrate the QMS in operation and the technical documentation, close any nonconformities, obtain the certificate, finalise the Declaration of Conformity, affix CE marking and transition into operate-and-maintain (PMS, vigilance, surveillance audits).

steps.t: Get the team and evidence ready
steps.d: make sure the people who actually run each process are available and can speak to their work, and that records are findable in minutes. Auditors talk to the doers, not just the QMR.
demonstrate the QMS working in practice across the audited processes, providing objective evidence on request. Show the real system, not a special show for the day.
if something isn't done, say so - don't bluff. Auditors find gaps faster than you can hide them, and a caught bluff damages trust in everything else.
correct and provide CAPA evidence for any Stage 2 nonconformities within the timeline the Notified Body sets - this is the last gate before the certificate.

steps.t: Get the certificate and CE mark
steps.d: on certificate issue, finalise the Declaration of Conformity, affix the CE marking, and complete EUDAMED registration. You may now place the device on the EU market.

move into steady state: keep PMS and vigilance running, and prepare for the annual surveillance audits and eventual recertification. Certification is the start of the maintained state, not the finish line.

steps.t: The everyday QMS is demonstrable on site and matches the documentation.
Audit findings are addressed within the required timeframe.
A plan for ongoing surveillance, PMS and recertification is in place.

records: Final Declaration of Conformity and CE-marking / registration records
tips: Stage 2 audits the system in PRACTICE, not on paper. The doers must be able to speak to their work - a QMS that only the QMR understands fails here.
Never bluff an auditor. 'We haven't fully implemented that yet' is recoverable; a caught exaggeration makes them doubt - and re-check - everything else you've said.
Show your real, everyday system, not a polished version staged for audit day. Auditors are very good at spotting a process that only exists for them.
The certificate is the START of the maintained state, not the end. Surveillance audits, PMS, vigilance and recertification continue for the life of the device - build for the long run.

lean.start: Demonstrate the real, everyday system honestly and close findings promptly to earn the certificate.
lean.evolve: Settle into a sustained operate-and-maintain rhythm: PMS, vigilance, surveillance audits and recertification.

subs.t: Demonstrate the everyday QMS on site
subs.why: Stage 2 checks that your real, day-to-day system matches the documentation. Demonstrating honestly is what earns the certificate.
subs.what: Show the auditor the QMS as it actually runs, matching the documentation.

how.t: Show the QMS as it really runs
how.d: Records and real practice, not just procedures.
People who know their work and can speak to it.
Auditors trust candour; spin breeds findings.
Fast retrieval signals control.

subs.why: Any findings from Stage 2 must be closed within the required timeframe to get or keep the certificate.
subs.what: Close the audit findings with corrective action within the required timeframe.

how.d: Real fixes, not patches.
Confirm each correction is effective.
To the Notified Body.
Findings must close within the required timeframe.

subs.why: Certification is the start of an operate-and-maintain cycle, not the finish line. Planning surveillance keeps you certified.
subs.what: Obtain the certificate and set up the ongoing surveillance, PMS and recertification rhythm.

how.d: QMS and/or device certification.
The NB's ongoing surveillance audits.
Plan the renewal well ahead.
The living obligations that keep you certified.

activities.why: If you make physical product - in-house or through a contract manufacturer - you need a controlled, repeatable way to build every unit to the released specification, with full traceability. This is where your design becomes a real product, and where an auditor checks that what you ship is genuinely what you designed and validated.
activities.what: Stand up your production process: adapt the production SOP to how you actually manufacture, write the work instructions and the device history record, then build under controlled conditions with lot/serial traceability and in-process checks before release.

steps.t: Adapt the production SOP to your scope
steps.d: Take PRO-SOP-01 and cut it to how you really make the product - in-house, fully outsourced, or a mix. If a contract manufacturer builds it, your SOP mainly governs how you control, monitor and release their work.

steps.t: Create the work instructions & device history record
steps.d: Use PRO-TPL-01/02/03 to write the build instructions and the production / device-history record that travels with each batch or unit.
Check the work order, approved instructions, materials, equipment status and operator competence before you start.
Build to the released specification, keeping identification and lot/serial traceability of materials and product at every step.
Perform the in-process controls at the defined points; record results against the acceptance criteria and segregate anything nonconforming.
Reconcile quantities, complete the device history record, have QC review it for completeness and conformity, and let the authorised person release or reject the batch.

steps.t: Each batch or unit has a completed device history record.
Lot/serial traceability is demonstrable from any finished unit.

tips: Outsourcing manufacturing does not outsource responsibility - you still own release and traceability; nail it down in a quality agreement with the maker.
Traceability is what auditors test hardest: take any finished unit and you should be able to trace every material, step and check back from it.
Don't write instructions you won't follow - a short instruction that matches reality beats a detailed one that doesn't.

lean.start: One clear work instruction and a one-page device-history record per build, with lot/serial traceability. That is already a controlled production process.
lean.evolve: Add MES/ERP tooling, statistical process control, automated traceability and supplier scorecards as volume and the number of products grow.

subs.t: Set up the production process & records
subs.why: A controlled, repeatable build process is where your design becomes a real product with traceability.
subs.what: Adapt PRO-SOP-01 to how you manufacture and create the work instructions and device history record.

how.d: In-house, outsourced or mixed manufacture.
Step-by-step build instructions operators actually follow.
The DHR that travels with each unit and captures what was done.
Identification at each step so any unit can be traced back.

subs.why: Building to the released spec with in-process checks is what keeps every unit conforming.
subs.what: Manufacture to spec with identification, traceability and the defined in-process controls.

how.d: Work order, materials, equipment and competent operators in place.
Maintaining identification and traceability throughout.
At the defined control points.
Stop and quarantine anything failing a check.

subs.why: Independent review and authorised release is the gate that stops a flawed batch reaching a patient.
subs.what: Complete the device history record, have QC review it, and let the authorised person release or reject.

how.d: Reconcile quantities and finish the DHR.
An independent check of the record and product.
Only a named, authorised person decides - and it is recorded.
Product is not shipped before release is signed.

activities.why: Some manufacturing steps - sterile sealing, moulding, welding, automated assembly - cannot be fully checked by inspecting the finished unit. For those, you must prove up front that the process consistently produces conforming product. Skip this and you are shipping on hope, which an auditor will not accept.
activities.what: Identify the processes that can't be verified by later inspection, then qualify them through IQ/OQ/PQ against pre-agreed acceptance criteria, and define when revalidation is triggered.

steps.d: Take PRO-SOP-03 and list which of your processes genuinely need validation (output not fully verifiable). Don't validate processes you can simply inspect.
Use PRO-TPL-06 to define scope, approach, responsibilities and acceptance criteria before you touch the equipment.
Verify the equipment is installed and configured to specification.
Challenge the process across its operating ranges against the acceptance criteria.
Demonstrate consistent conforming output under real production conditions, with a documented statistical rationale for sampling.
Use PRO-TPL-07 for the validation summary; the QMR approves it and releases the process for routine production.
Define when a change or interval forces revalidation, and record the assessment each time.

tips: Acceptance criteria must be set before you run the qualification, not chosen afterwards to fit the result.
Vendor IQ/OQ packs are a head start, not the whole job - your PQ has to reflect your product and your conditions.
A process change after validation without a revalidation assessment is one of the most common audit findings here.

lean.start: Validate only the one or two processes you genuinely cannot inspect, to a depth matched to their risk, leaning on vendor IQ/OQ where you can.
lean.evolve: Add continuous process verification, broader statistical sampling and periodic revalidation cycles as production scales.

subs.why: Validate only what you cannot fully inspect - identify those processes and write the master plan first.
subs.what: Identify the processes needing validation and write the validation master plan with acceptance criteria.

how.d: Those whose output cannot be fully verified by later inspection (welding, moulding, sterilization...).
Scope, approach and responsibilities.
Before running anything.
How many runs/units, with a rationale.

subs.why: IQ/OQ/PQ proves the process is installed right, operates across its range, and consistently produces conforming output.
subs.what: Run installation, operational and performance qualification against the acceptance criteria.

how.d: Equipment installed and configured to specification.
Challenged across the operating ranges and worst cases.
Consistent conforming output under real production conditions.
Objective evidence against the criteria.

subs.why: Approval releases the process; revalidation triggers keep it valid as things change.
subs.what: Approve the validation summary and define when revalidation is triggered.

how.d: Release the process for routine use.
Equipment/material/process change, or a periodic interval.
Routine data showing it stays in a validated state.
The assessment and its outcome.

activities.why: If your device is supplied sterile, sterility and the sterile barrier are safety-critical and cannot be inspected into the product - they have to be validated and controlled every batch. This activity only applies if you actually sterilize; if you don't, record that as a justified non-application and move on.
activities.what: Adopt your sterilization and packaging controls, validate the sterilization process and the sterile-barrier packaging, and control every routine cycle with release records.

steps.d: Take STE-SOP-01, choose your method (EO, radiation, steam, or outsourced) and scope the controls. If you outsource sterilization, your SOP governs the contractor and the incoming/outgoing checks.
Use STE-TPL-01/02 to specify the sterile-barrier system and the sterilization method and parameters.
Validate the sterilization process (to the relevant ISO 11135/11137/17665) and the packaging seal integrity and shelf life (EN ISO 11607).
Run every batch to the validated parameters, with cycle monitoring and the release records on STE-FOR-01.
Release sterile product only against the cycle records and required indicators/test results; segregate any failed cycle.

steps.t: Sterilization and the sterile-barrier packaging are validated.
Each sterile batch is released on its cycle records.

tips: Packaging validation is not optional - a validated sterilization with an unproven seal still fails the patient.
Outsourced sterilization still needs your oversight: a quality agreement, parameter review and release on the contractor's records.
Shelf-life / ageing evidence takes real time - start it early or it becomes your launch bottleneck.

lean.start: If you outsource sterilization: a quality agreement, the contractor's validation, and release on their cycle records. That is a controlled sterile process.
lean.evolve: Bring monitoring in-house, add dosimetric release and richer ageing studies as volume and product range grow.

subs.why: If you supply sterile, sterility and the sterile barrier are safety-critical and must be controlled deliberately.
subs.what: Adapt STE-SOP-01, choose the method, and specify the sterile-barrier packaging.

how.t: Adapt the SOP & choose the method
how.d: EO, radiation, steam or outsourced - matched to your device.
The packaging that maintains sterility to point of use.
Manufacturing cleanliness feeding the sterilization.
A quality agreement if outsourced.

subs.why: Both the sterilization process and the packaging seal must be validated - a sterile product in an unproven barrier still fails.
subs.what: Validate the sterilization process and the packaging seal integrity and shelf life.

how.d: To the relevant ISO 11135 (EO) / 11137 (radiation) / 17665 (steam).
Seal integrity and strength (EN ISO 11607).
Real-time and accelerated ageing - start it early, it takes real time.
Evidence the SAL is met.

subs.why: Routine control and release on cycle records is what keeps every batch sterile, not just the validated one.
subs.what: Run every batch to validated parameters with monitoring and release on the cycle records.

how.t: Run every cycle to validated parameters
how.d: No deviation from the validated recipe.
Parameters and indicators on the cycle record (STE-FOR-01).
Only release batches whose cycle met the criteria.
Quarantine and investigate.

activities.why: If you make decisions about product on a measurement - a gauge, a tester, a calibrated jig - then a drifting instrument quietly passes bad product or rejects good product. Calibration is how you trust your own measurements, and auditors check it because it underpins every other check you make.
activities.what: Set up a simple calibration system: list the equipment that affects product conformity, calibrate it against traceable standards on a schedule, and control what happens when something is found out of calibration.

steps.d: Take INF-SOP-02 and scope it to the instruments that actually affect product decisions - not every tool in the building.
List each item in INF-LIS-03 with its calibration interval, range and traceable reference standard.
Calibrate or verify each instrument against a traceable standard before use and at the defined interval; record results on INF-FOR-01.
Mark calibration status so anyone can see an instrument is in date, and protect instruments from adjustment that would invalidate it.
If an instrument is found out of calibration, assess and record the impact on product already measured with it, and act on anything affected.

steps.t: A calibration register lists the due date for each instrument that affects product.
Out-of-tolerance findings trigger an impact assessment on product already measured.

refs: 7.6 Control of monitoring and measuring equipment

tips: The out-of-tolerance impact assessment is the step people forget - a failed instrument means questioning everything it measured since it last passed.
Calibrate to traceable standards (national/ISO), and keep the certificate - 'we checked it ourselves' is not traceability.
Scope it tightly: software-only products usually have little or none of this; record that rather than inventing it.

lean.start: A spreadsheet of the few instruments that affect product, with due dates, plus their calibration certificates in a folder.
lean.evolve: Add calibration-management software, automated due-date alerts and in-house calibration capability as the equipment base grows.

subs.why: Calibration is how you trust your own measurements - start with the instruments that actually affect product decisions.
subs.what: Adapt INF-SOP-02 and list the relevant instruments with intervals and traceable references.

how.t: Scope to instruments that decide product
how.d: Only those whose measurement affects a product decision need calibration.
Each instrument with range, interval and reference standard (INF-LIS-03).
And make overdue items visible.
References traceable to national/international standards.

subs.why: Calibrating against traceable standards and labelling status keeps drift from quietly passing bad product.
subs.what: Calibrate or verify each instrument against a traceable standard on schedule and mark its status.

how.d: On schedule, keeping the certificate.
What in-tolerance means for each instrument.

activities.why: Where you build matters: dust, static, humidity, handling and cleanliness can damage product or compromise sterility before it ever reaches a patient. You only need controls proportionate to your product - a clean bench for some, a cleanroom for others, very little for software - but you must decide and control it deliberately.
activities.what: Define the work-environment and cleanliness requirements your product needs, put proportionate controls and monitoring in place, and keep the records that show conditions stay within limits.

steps.d: Take INF-SOP-03 and decide what your product actually requires - cleanliness, ESD, temperature/humidity, gowning, health and hygiene of staff.
Document the environmental requirements and how each is monitored, with the limits that matter.
Put the controls in place (cleaning regime, ESD measures, access, gowning) and monitor conditions at the defined frequency.
Keep the monitoring records that show the environment stays within limits during production.

tips: Match the control to the risk - a cleanroom you can't maintain is worse than a clean bench you can.

lean.start: A short note of what your product needs (e.g. a clean, static-safe bench) plus a simple cleaning and handling routine you actually follow.
lean.evolve: Add controlled/clean rooms, continuous environmental monitoring and gowning regimes as product sensitivity and volume grow.

subs.why: Where you build matters - decide the cleanliness, ESD and handling controls your product actually needs, proportionate to risk.

activities.why: Sooner or later something contaminated turns up - a return from the field, a spill, a suspected-infected unit. You need a safe, pre-agreed way to contain it so it protects people and can never quietly re-enter usable stock. Without it, a single contaminated return can put staff at risk and pollute good inventory.
activities.what: Adopt a procedure to identify, segregate and safely handle contaminated or suspect product, decide its disposition, and stop it re-entering use.

steps.d: Take PSA-SOP-01 and tailor it to your realistic sources of contamination (field returns, production spills, servicing).
Immediately identify, physically segregate and clearly label affected product as contaminated / quarantined.
Assess the nature, extent and risk; obtain authorisation for decontamination, rework or controlled disposal.
Carry out the decontamination or disposal so that contaminated product cannot re-enter usable stock, and protect the people doing it.
Record the event and route any systemic cause into CAPA before closing it out.

tips: The non-negotiable rule: contaminated returns can never re-join usable stock - a separate, labelled quarantine area enforces that.
Tell staff how to receive and handle a contaminated return safely before one arrives, not after.
Recurring contamination is a CAPA trigger, not just a clean-up job.

lean.start: A labelled quarantine bin/area and a one-page rule for receiving and handling a suspect return safely.
lean.evolve: Add decontamination validation, dedicated handling areas and trend analysis of contamination events as field volume grows.

activities.why: If your device is electrically active or connects to mains or patient circuits, electrical and energy safety is a basic essential requirement - get it wrong and you can harm a patient directly. This is design-verification work: you prove safety against the recognised standard before you ship.
activities.what: Plan and run the active/electrical safety testing your device needs against IEC 60601-1 and the relevant collateral and particular standards, and keep the evidence in your technical file.

steps.d: Take AES-SOP-01 and scope it to your device's energy types (mains, battery, applied parts, RF).
Pin down the IEC 60601-1 edition plus the right collateral (-1-x) and particular (-2-xx) standards for your device type.
Use AES-TPL-01 to define the test plan and acceptance criteria.
Test in-house or at an accredited lab; record results on AES-FOR-01.
Log the test reports in AES-LIS-01 and link them to your GSPR / essential-requirements evidence.

tips: Accredited-lab slots book up - schedule electrical safety testing early or it delays your whole launch.
A design change after testing can invalidate it; assess the re-test need under change control.
The general standard alone is rarely enough - identify the particular standard (60601-2-xx) for your device type.

lean.start: For a low-energy device: the applicable standard's required tests run once at an accredited lab, plus the declaration, filed.
lean.evolve: Add in-house pre-compliance testing and broader particular-standard coverage as the product range grows.

activities.why: If any part of your device touches the patient - skin, mucosa, blood, tissue - the materials themselves can cause harm. Biological evaluation is how you show, risk-based, that body contact is safe. It only applies if you have patient contact; if not, record that and move on.
activities.what: Run a risk-based biological evaluation per ISO 10993-1: categorise the body contact, assess each biological endpoint from materials and existing data, and test only where a real gap remains.

steps.d: Take BIO-SOP-01 and categorise the nature and duration of body contact for your device.
Use BIO-TPL-01 to map the biological endpoints by contact category; gather material, supplier and literature data before testing.
Commission biological tests only where evidence is missing; record results on BIO-FOR-01.
Write the biological evaluation report (BIO-TPL-02) and log the studies in BIO-LIS-01.

tips: Biocompatibility is risk-based, not a fixed test list - chemical characterisation plus existing data can close many endpoints without animal testing.
Material or supplier changes can reopen the evaluation - tie it to change control.
Start early: biological testing has long lead times and can gate your submission.

lean.start: An evaluation plan that leans on material data and literature, testing only the genuine gaps.
lean.evolve: Add chemical characterisation, toxicological risk assessment and broader endpoint coverage as materials and claims expand.

activities.why: If your device incorporates a medicinal substance (a drug-device combination), you carry extra obligations - the medicinal part must be assessed, often with a medicines authority consulted. Skip this and your conformity route is invalid.
activities.what: Identify the combination-product status, apply the right controls for the medicinal constituent, and manage the consultation/assessment route (e.g. EU MDR Article 117).

steps.d: Take CMB-SOP-01 and confirm the combination-product classification and route.
Document the medicinal part's quality, safety and benefit.

activities.why: If you make devices to a specific practitioner's prescription for an individual patient (custom-made or patient-matched), you follow a distinct conformity route with its own statement and records - not the standard CE route. Using the wrong route is a compliance failure.
activities.what: Set up the custom-made device controls: prescription handling, the Annex XIII statement, per-device records and patient traceability.

steps.d: Take CMD-SOP-01 and confirm custom-made vs patient-matched status and the applicable route.
Record the practitioner's prescription and manufacture to it.
Issue the Annex XIII statement (CMD-TPL-01) for each device.

tips: Custom-made is not a shortcut around safety - GSPRs still apply, only the conformity route differs.

activities.statement: Establish the IVD design & development process
activities.why: An in-vitro diagnostic is developed around its intended purpose and its analytical and clinical performance - what it measures, in whom, and how well. This activity stands up the IVD-specific design process so your performance claims are designed in and provable, not bolted on.
activities.what: Run the IVD design & development process: define the intended purpose and performance specification, design the assay/system under design controls, and plan the analytical and clinical performance studies.

steps.d: Take DEV-SOP-02 and write the intended-purpose and performance specification (DEV-TPL-03) - analyte, matrix, population, use.
Develop the assay/system with design inputs/outputs and reviews.
Plan analytical performance (DEV-TPL-04) and scientific validity / clinical performance (DEV-TPL-05).
Run analytical studies (precision, LoD, interference) and record results on DEV-FOR-02/03.
Finalise the performance specification and design outputs (DEV-TPL-06); log studies on DEV-FOR-04/05.

steps.t: Analytical performance (precision, LoD, interference) is documented.

tips: For an IVD the intended purpose drives everything - pin down analyte, matrix, population and use before you design.
Analytical performance is your foundation; weak analytical data sinks the clinical claims.
Specimen handling and stability are part of the design, not an afterthought.

lean.start: A clear intended-purpose statement and an analytical performance plan tied to your design inputs.
lean.evolve: Add full clinical performance studies and broader matrix/interference coverage as the assay matures.

activities.why: IVDs carry a particular risk: a wrong result leads to a wrong clinical decision. IVD risk management (ISO 14971 applied to diagnostics) makes you think through false positives and negatives and the harm they cause downstream, not just device failure.
activities.what: Set up IVD-specific risk management: identify diagnostic hazards (incorrect or delayed results), assess the clinical consequence, and control them through design and information.

steps.d: Take RSK-SOP-02 and agree your acceptability criteria for false results.
Use RSK-TPL-06 covering analytical errors, user errors and result-interpretation harm; define the controls.
Capture residual risk and benefit-risk in the report (RSK-TPL-07), linked to your performance evidence.

tips: For IVDs the dominant hazard is usually a wrong or late result, not the box breaking - your hazard analysis must reflect that.
Tie risk controls to your analytical and clinical performance data - the numbers justify acceptability.
Keep IVD risk and the main risk file consistent; one feeds the other.

lean.start: One risk table focused on incorrect/delayed results and their clinical harm, with the obvious controls.

activities.why: Even after design, each manufactured lot of an IVD can drift - reagent lots, calibrators, controls. Production-phase risk control keeps lot-to-lot performance within limits so the result a clinician gets is trustworthy every time.
activities.what: Control production-phase risk for the IVD: lot-release criteria, calibrator/control traceability and ongoing performance monitoring - or record a justified non-application if you do not manufacture.

steps.d: Take RSK-SOP-03 and scope it to your manufacturing - or record a justified non-application.
Define lot-release performance criteria and calibrator/control value-assignment with metrological traceability.
Monitor lot-to-lot performance and act on drift.

Watch lot-to-lot drift actively; a slow shift in controls is an early warning, not noise.

activities.why: An IVD's CE evidence is its performance evaluation - the proof of scientific validity, analytical performance and clinical performance for the intended purpose. It is the IVDR equivalent of clinical evaluation, and it is what a notified body scrutinises hardest.
activities.what: Compile the IVD performance evaluation: the plan, the three pillars of evidence, and the performance evaluation report, kept live through post-market performance follow-up.

activities.why: Once your IVD is in use, real-world performance can differ from the lab. Post-market surveillance and performance follow-up (PMPF) catch drift, misuse and rare failures and feed them back into your evidence and risk file - an IVDR legal duty.
activities.what: Stand up IVD post-market surveillance and PMPF: collect field performance data on a plan, analyse it, and update the performance evaluation and risk file.

steps.d: Take PMS-SOP-02 and write the PMS / PMPF plan (PMS-TPL-05).

tips: PMPF is proactive - waiting for complaints is not performance follow-up.
Close the loop: PMS findings must visibly update your performance evaluation and risk file, or auditors flag a broken loop.

activities.why: Software always ships with known issues and discovers new ones. IEC 62304 requires a disciplined problem-resolution process so every defect is recorded, risk-assessed and fixed in a controlled, traceable way - especially the safety-relevant ones.
activities.what: Set up the software problem-resolution process: capture problem reports, evaluate them (including safety/risk), resolve them under change control, and verify the fixes.

steps.t: Each software anomaly is risk-assessed and traced to a verified fix.

activities.why: Getting software safely from 'done' to 'in the field' - and keeping it safe through updates - is its own controlled process. IEC 62304 covers release, deployment and maintenance so that what users run is the version you verified, and updates do not introduce new risk.
activities.what: Establish software release, deployment and maintenance: controlled builds and release records, deployment to users, and a maintenance process for updates and patches.

steps.d: Take DEP-SOP-01 and define your release and deployment controls (DEP-TPL-01).
Produce the release with its configuration/version record (DEP-TPL-02), verify it, and deploy under control (DEP-FOR-01).

steps.t: The released version matches the verified build.

activities.statement: Establish the US FDA market pathway & release
activities.why: Selling in the USA means FDA, not CE. You have to determine the right pathway (510(k), De Novo, PMA or exempt), make the submission, and only distribute once cleared or approved - with US-specific QMSR and UDI obligations on top.
activities.what: Establish the US market route: determine the FDA pathway, compile and submit the application, and confirm cleared/approved status with registration, listing, QMSR and UDI in place.

steps.d: Take TEF-SOP-01 and determine classification and pathway (TEF-TPL-01).

Build the submission (substantial-equivalence for a 510(k), or clinical/technical evidence for a PMA, plus FDA cybersecurity docs for software); submit and respond to FDA questions; record on TEF-FOR-01/02.
Confirm clearance/approval before US distribution; complete establishment registration and device listing; log status in TEF-LIS-01 and reassess on significant change.

steps.t: The FDA pathway is determined and documented.

tips: Don't ship to the US before clearance/approval - and confirm QMSR (21 CFR 820) and UDI are in place first.

lean.start: A clear pathway determination and a well-organised submission, with registration and listing ready to file on clearance.
lean.evolve: Add a US agent relationship, pre-submission (Q-Sub) meetings and a portfolio submission strategy as you scale.

activities.why: Once on the US market you must report serious adverse events (MDR, 21 CFR 803) on the clock and manage corrections and removals (recalls, 21 CFR 806). Miss a reporting deadline and you have a regulatory violation, regardless of the underlying event.
activities.what: Establish US adverse-event reporting and field-action management: assess reportability, file MDRs on time, and run corrections/removals with FDA notification.

steps.d: Take VIG-SOP-01 and define the reportability decision and the reporting clocks.
Assess each event against 21 CFR 803 and file the MDR (eMDR / MedWatch 3500A) within the timeframe - 30 days, or 5 where remedial action is required; record on VIG-FOR-01.
Decide and classify any correction/removal, report under 21 CFR 806 where required, execute it, and feed findings into CAPA and PMS.

steps.t: MDR reportability is assessed against 21 CFR 803 with the clock logged.

tips: The reporting clock starts when you become aware - log the date and don't let assessment eat the deadline.

A correction or removal may be reportable under 806 even if you call it a 'field update' - assess it.

1.q: Which document must be established FIRST, before any other QMS document?
1.e: Document & record control is the foundation every other activity relies on - activity P1.1.

1.q: Two activities sharing the same 'Wave' within a phase means they:
a: Must be done in strict sequence
1.e: A wave groups activities with no dependency between them, so they can run in parallel.

1.q: What is the focus of Phase 1?
a: Prepare for the Notified Body audit
Ensure the core processes operate correctly & safely
1.e: Phase 1 gets the primary process running correctly and safely before anything else.

1.q: ISO 13485 clause 5 makes which of these an explicit requirement, not optional good practice?
a: Management commitment, a quality policy, measurable objectives and a management representative
1.e: Clause 5 requires top-management commitment, the quality policy, measurable objectives and a named QMR (P1.2).

a: The QMS scope, the interaction of processes and any justified exclusions
1.e: ISO 13485 4.2.2 - the manual frames the scope, process interactions and any exclusions (P1.4).

1.q: Under ISO 13485 6.2, staff whose work affects quality must be competent on the basis of:
1.e: Competence is evidence-based: education, training, skills and experience (P1.5).

1.q: Why is design & development planning a critical-path activity in Phase 1?
a: It is the backbone of the technical file and feeds Phase 2's design controls
1.e: Planned D&D is where safety and performance are built in - it drives the technical file (P1.7).

2.q: The integrated risk file opened in Phase 2 covers which branches?
2.e: One integrated file: ISO 14971 (safety) + AAMI TIR57 (security) + EU AI Act Art 9 (AI) - activity P2.1.

a: Inputs traced to outputs and to verification
2.e: Design inputs -> outputs -> verification & validation is the chain an auditor follows.

2.q: Which lifecycle runs in PARALLEL with general design controls in Phase 2?
2.e: Software development (IEC 62304) runs alongside design controls - same wave.

2.q: ISO 14971 requires risk controls to be applied in which order of priority?
a: Inherent safety by design, then protective measures, then information for safety
2.e: The risk-control hierarchy is inherent safe design, then protective measures, then information for safety (P2.1).

2.q: IEC 62366-1 usability engineering is required because most device incidents are caused by:
2.e: Use error is the leading source of harm, so usability engineering must prove safe use (P2.4).

2.q: Under the EU AI Act, an AI-based medical device is typically classified as:
2.e: Medical-device AI is usually high-risk, triggering data governance, transparency, oversight and robustness duties (P2.7).

2.q: A corrective action (CAPA) may only be closed once you have:
2.e: ISO 13485 8.5.2 requires verification of effectiveness before a CAPA is closed (P2.10).

3.q: Which governance activity is established in Phase 3?
3.e: Phase 3 stands up internal audit, management review and the policy cycle.

3.q: Management review must be driven by:
a: Analysed quality, security and AI data
3.e: Top management steers the QMS using analysed data and trends.

3.q: What is the focus of Phase 3?
3.e: Phase 3 makes the system governed and led.

a: The owner of the audited process
Objective - they must not audit their own work
3.e: ISO 13485 8.2.4 requires objective auditors who do not audit their own work (P3.1).

3.q: In business continuity planning, RTO and RPO define:
a: The maximum tolerable downtime and data loss
3.e: Recovery Time / Recovery Point Objectives bound acceptable downtime and data loss (P3.4).

3.q: Why must the medical device file be kept continuously current?
a: Because regulations, standards and guidance change and conformity must stay demonstrated
3.e: Regulatory monitoring keeps the technical documentation conformant as requirements evolve (P3.3).

4.q: In which phase is the Declaration of Conformity issued?
4.e: Compiling the technical file and issuing the DoC is the market gate in Phase 4 (P4.3).

4.q: The Notified Body conformity assessment is typically split into:
4.e: Stage 1 checks documentation readiness; Stage 2 audits effective operation on-site (P4.6, P4.7).

4.q: FMEA on all processes belongs to which Quality System Essential?
4.e: FMEA is a continual-improvement activity that prevents nonconformities (Phase 4).

4.q: For an IVD, the counterpart of the MDR clinical evaluation is the:
4.e: IVDs require a performance evaluation - scientific validity, analytical and clinical performance (P4.1).

a: Vigilance, the risk file, the clinical evaluation and CAPA
4.e: PMS closes the loop into vigilance, the risk file, the clinical evaluation and CAPA (P4.2).

4.q: In the technical file, the GSPR checklist must:
a: Link every general safety & performance requirement to its evidence
4.e: Each general safety and performance requirement is mapped to traceable evidence (P4.3).

4.q: The manufacturer may affix the CE marking only after:
a: The Notified Body issues the certificate
4.e: CE marking follows certificate issue at the end of Stage 2 (P4.7).
